text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: What support does the ASU Library provide for open access publishing?
Answer: ASU Library can provide waivers or discounts on APCs with certain publishers as a result of our partnerships and subscriptions. Additionally, we manage the ASU Open Access (OA) Publishing Fund, which supports ASU authors who wish to make their scholarly articles freely available immediately upon publication. All current ASU faculty, staff and students are eligible for funds, with a maximum award of $2000 per request. Applications to the fund will open in January 2026. For a complete current list of partnerships and the criteria and application instructions for the OA Publishing Fund, visit our Researcher Support open access publishing and support page. If making your work open access is important to you, you can publish in a traditional journal as long as you retain the rights to self-archive your work in a repository , such as our KEEP institutional repository or a disciplinary repository of choice.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: I would like to learn how to manage my research data throughout my project
Answer: Our Introduction to Research Data Management tutorial is the best place to start. It is a self-paced walkthrough of the essential concepts in research data management, including definitions, effective management, sharing strategies, and other tips to help keep you organized and track your process for reproducibility and recognition for your efforts. We also have a research data management guide , including support options, suggested readings, data management plan writing, and links to data management tutorials. The ASU Research Data Management Office also has additional information, mainly if you are working on a grant-funded project. After checking out these resources, let us know if you have any other questions and if you would like us to add you to an email list for potential workshops and events around research data management and sharing that we will hold in the future.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: I'm starting a group research project and have been asked to determine the best way to organize the research that will be done to provide continuity throughout the life of the project. Can you assist by recommending some information management tools?
Answer: It's great that your team is identifying their research management tools early in the project! There are many possible scenarios for working together as a team, so I'm going to share what I'd personally recommend investigating, but there are of course other options out there, depending on the preferences of your team members. First, I recommend Zotero as a great, collaborative citation manager with options to use as a team. Since it's open source, it's freely available to everyone across institutions, has an active user community and support , and plenty of tools - a web option, browser extensions, and a desktop client - to easily save and share your research review and format bibliographies and citations. There is a paid account option to add storage space, but if that's not in your team's budget, you can pair it with a shared Dropbox folder to store the pdfs or other files to keep the full text handy. Secondly, you might consider using OSF (Open Science Framework) as a project and information management tool for your team. You can sync a variety of storage and other tools in one place (OSF works with Zotero, Dropbox, Google Drive, and more). ASU is an institutional affiliate of OSF, so you can create accounts using your ASURITE log in information to make it easy to get started . OSF also has an active user community with a lot of guidance targeting research groups (for example, this getting started page ) The Citation Management Library Guide includes some other tools for managing your information - both Mendeley and Endnote are proprietary (rather than open source) tools, so will have more limitations on access and use depending on institutional affiliations of all your team members and subscription options. I personally prefer using open source tools when available and possible (and both OSF and Zotero are great tools!).

FAQ Question: What is ILLiad?
Answer: ​ ILLiad is the online platform used to manage your interlibrary loan requests.  You can use ILLiad to: Create an ILL account Update your user information, including address View electronically delivered items, such as articles Manage your email and text notifications Check the status of your requests And more!  Log in to your ILLiad account at illiad.lib.asu.edu

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: What is a DOI?
Answer: A DOI is a Digital Object Identifier. It is a unique number that is assigned to an object that provides a persistent link to the object. https://www.doi.org/ This often applies to scholarly journal articles, but can apply to other items. A DOI eliminates issues with changes made to an object’s URL (web address). All DOIs begin with a "10." In citation styles, if available, a DOI is usually shown at the end of the citation. DOIs are visible in some databases, but "hidden" in others. Not every item nor journal article has a DOI. If you have a DOI, you can try our library Citation Linker to see if we have the article. Citation Linker Here is a link to our Find Articles page.

FAQ Question: Who can use interlibrary loan services through ASU Library?
Answer: For additional information about ASU Library's Interlibrary Loan service, please visit our guide about using Interlibrary Loan ! Current ASU students, staff, and faculty can make interlibrary loan requests through ASU Library using your Interlibrary Loan ILLiad account . Some ASU students, staff, and faculty may have items delivered to their home addresses instead of an ASU Library. Sandra Day O’Connor College of Law students, faculty and staff can make interlibrary loan requests through ASU’s Ross-Blakley Law Library .

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Why are so many library items located off site in the High Density Collection facility?
Answer: To make room for student study spaces and other library services, we have relocated many books to our High Density Collection (HDC) facility, where we already store millions of volumes. HDC includes a patron request fulfillment center, and staff are able to pull books and deliver them to ASU campuses, often within a few hours, and usually within a day or two. Please feel free to use the "Request ASU Copy" link in the library item record to request most of these items. There is no limit on these requests for our ASU affiliates.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Can someone else pick up my interlibrary loan items for me, on my behalf?
Answer: Yes, ASU students, faculty and staff may authorize another individual, as a “proxy”, to use their Library account to transact library business in their name. Community card borrowers are not eligible for this service. How to apply To apply for a proxy card, visit our Proxy Accounts page and print the application form. Conditions and agreements It is important to remember the following conditions when requesting this service: The named individual has your permission to use your library account to transact library business in your name. You are responsible for all library materials checked out using your ASU Library account.  This includes proper return to one of the ASU Library and any fines incurred. Proxies must present their own photo ID card and indicate that they are borrowing materials under Proxy at time of checkout. The status of all proxies is reviewed annually in May. Proxy status for all Teaching Assistants expires in May. To renew, submit a new form. You may give someone who is not a designated proxy permission to check out library materials with your ASU ID card. To do so, please have them present their own ID, your ASU ID and a signed, dated, written permission from you. Questions? Please contact the Circulation Department at circ@asu.edu or 480-965-3605 with any questions. More information: https://lib.asu.edu/access/proxy

FAQ Question: How do I access the Bloomberg service in the library?
Answer: The Bloomberg Professional Service uses a dedicated computer (no remote access) to provide near real-time access to information on financial markets worldwide. Location: Noble Library 2nd floor, Room 276 Access: Access is by appointment only . ASU students, faculty and staff ONLY! To schedule an appointment, see our Bloomberg guide . Select the guide's calendar link to make an appointment. Check the guide for additional information about using Bloomberg resources!

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: I am enrolled in ASU Pathway to Admissions program. Do I have access to all library resources?
Answer: Instructors for Earned Admission Learners will provide the resources necessary for students to do their work. Learners will not have access to library resources until they have been admitted to ASU and are registered for at least one credit-earning course. ASU for You is a great place to explore publicly accessible digital learning materials, including books, articles, and other resources available to the public.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: What is a microform?
Answer: Microforms are scaled-down reproductions of documents, journal issues, monographs, or schemata made for the purposes of transmission, storage, reading, printing, and scanning. Reading, printing, and scanning microforms requires a special reader (or software). Readers and software are available at select ASU Library locations . ASU Library owns microforms in the following formats: microfilm ( film reels) microfiche (film sheets) micro-opaque (stock cards)

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: The citation I have for an article might not be correct. What do I do now?
Answer: If searching the internet doesn't help you discover the correct citation... Here are a few things to try within our library systems: Make sure you are signed in to the library (top right corner of the library home page ). Near the center of the home page, choose one of the following options from the drop down list to the right of the large search bar: One Search Then type or paste the title of the article into the search bar. Click SEARCH. If your article is not in the first few results, try a different method. Google Scholar Then type or paste the title of the article into the search bar. Click SEARCH. If your article is not in the first few results, try a different method. Journal Title Then type or paste the name of the journal into the search bar. Click SEARCH. Select the result, if any, for the online version of the journal. If there is a link to the publisher's website , click down the list in the following order until you get to the Table of Contents at the Issue level: Year Volume Issue Look through the Table of Contents listing in page number order. Note: The article you need may actually fall within the pages of a different article. This happens frequently with book reviews!! Open the full text and look for the starting page of your article, just in case it is actually within another article! Another way to search for the correct citation... On the library home page, scroll down and click the View Resources icon. In the drop down list, choose: Web of Science Try the Cited Reference Search Try last name of the Author and the name of the Journal Note: Web of Science indexes the reference lists from articles in various journals and books, but it does NOT check to see if the citations are correct! So... If you find variations of the citation, the correct one usually has the highest citation count! The others typically have a mistake in one or more of the following: year, volume, issue and/or page number. If you need help, try our Ask a Librarian services!

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Technical support...details that help us troubleshoot your technical issues...
Answer: When submitting a ticket about a technical problem with ASU Library resources , please include as much detail as possible. The following information is especially helpful: Are you at home, at work, or on campus when the difficulty occurs? Are you logged into your ASUrite account? What Operating System are you using? (Windows 8, macOS 10.12, etc.) What Browser and Version Number are you using? (Internet Explorer 11, Firefox 52.2.1esr, etc.) Description of the steps you followed and at which screen the problem or error message appeared What is the URL (http://...) at the point of the error? Exact wording of any error messages Any screenshots that you may think would be helpful A telephone number where you may be reached during business hours In the meantime, here are a few things to try: Switch to a different browser (one may work better than another for a particular database or application: Firefox, Chrome, Internet Explorer, etc.) If you are coming in through MyASU, try starting from the library home page https://lib.asu.edu and then make sure you are signed in at the top right corner of the library page.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Where can I find a permanent link to an item I found in One Search?
Answer: To find the persistent URL or permalink for a record you found in One Search... In the short display The short display is usually part of your results list as a numbered item (such as the number 3 item in your results list). Along the top right of the short display for the record, look for and click the three dots icon (...). In the resulting list of action options, click on "permalink" to display a URL that you can copy and paste for future use . In the full record display From the short record in the results list, click the title of the item until you find the longer full record for the item.  If there were multiple formats listed in the short record, you may need to click on the title in the new numbered list of short records, and then click on the title of an item to get to the full record! Look for the "Send to" section of the record to find the permalink. Then click on "permalink" to display a URL that you can copy and paste for future use.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Is there a way to search by an exact year within One Search?
Answer: Yes, you can search by exact year! Go to the ASU Library home page If you are a current ASU student, faculty or staff, Sign In at the top right corner of the library home page; this will give you more retrieval and full text options. Try our library's One Search : Enter keywords, title or author and click: Search On the results page, use the filters (on the right side of the page) to limit to a particular date range under: Publication Date Try our Advanced Search: Just below and to the right of the One Search bar, click: Advanced Search Use the Publication Date drop down on the bottom right side of the form to select: Any year specific date Last year Last 2 years Last 5 years Last 10 years Last 20 years

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: I need an award winning children's book! How can I find one?
Answer: Need an award winning Children's or Young Adult book? To identify award-winning children’s books... Search: Database of Award Winning Children's Literature It is searchable by: Award Author, Illustrator Age of reader Language Topic, Historical period Type of book (picture, chapter, etc.). To see if we have a copy: Strategy #1: Search the library's One Search or ASU Library Catalog . Start at lib.asu.edu Note: Try searching by the title of the book! Strategy #2: Search in One Search for "Caldecott" or "Newberry" Using the "Active Filters" select "Children and Young Adult" from the "Collection" heading Need more info about this topic? Our librarians have designed the Children’s & Young Adult Literature library guide to help you!

FAQ Question: ASU Budget and ASU Salary
Answer: ASU Salary data online AZ Data Central's Arizona government salary database is available on the AZ Central news website. This includes salaries for Arizona public employees, including community colleges and the three state universities - ASU, NAU, UA - for 2018. Their 2019 update is only available to their subscribers. ASU Salary information (starting from 2012) can also be found at the State Press website. ASU Salary books Printed copies are no longer published. Existing print copies are listed in the Library Catalog. Titles vary. Locations are typically High Density Storage Collection (HDC) - Stacks OR High Density Storage Collection (HDC) - Archival Collections. Requesting access to printed copies... Request copies from High Density Storage Collection (HDC) - Stacks In-house use. These may also be available for 7-day checkout to current ASU faculty, staff or students. Request copies from High Density Storage Collection (HDC) - Archival Collections through the Ask An Archivist form online for in-house use by appointment. For previous years, check each catalog record for "earlier title" listing. Then search catalog by the earlier title. Request HDC Stacks copy, when available. Arizona State University ... Operating Budget 2016/2017 Arizona State University ... state and local operating budget book 2014/2015, 2015/2016 Arizona State University Tempe/Downtown Phoenix/Polytechnic/West ... state and local operating budge t 2009/2010 - 2013/2014 Local operating budget : Tempe/Downtown Phoenix/Polytechnic/West 2008/2009 Local operating budget by organizational area 1997/1998 - 2007/2008 ASU Budget online The most current online information about ASU Budget can be found at cfo.asu.edu . ABOR, NAU, UA and Thunderbird (archives) Budgets online Arizona Board of Regents - Operational and Financial Review (OFR) NAU budget reports UA budget reports Thunderbird Archives libguide Contact the Thunderbird Archivist, listed in the guide for questions regarding the old Institute.

FAQ Question: How do I get technical help with ASU Library resources?
Answer: Technical help (ASU Library) If you are experiencing technical problems with any library online resource, such as broken links, catalog errors, database* problems, etc. and your attempts to troubleshoot the issue have failed, You can request help at: https://lib.asu.edu/systems/help . Sign in with your ASURITE ID and password Fill out and submit the form One of our technical support staff will respond via email to you as soon as possible. *For "400 Bad Request: Request Header or Cookie Too Large" errors when trying to access an article or database , please try the fixes listed in that FAQ before submitting a ticket.

FAQ Question: What is ASU Library’s Home Delivery Program, and how does it work?
Answer: The Home Delivery Program allows ASU students, faculty, and staff to request most physical ASU Library materials for delivery to their home address via FedEx shipping. For those on Native Lands or Rural Routes, materials are shipped through the United States Postal Service. Home Delivery service is available to ASU affiliates who live outside the Phoenix metropolitan/Maricopa County area but within the continental United States, Alaska, Hawaii, and Puerto Rico . Requesting items: When requesting ASU Library materials through the ASU Library website, please select "Personal Delivery" from the dropdown menu. Enter your complete home address for each item requested. Shipping details: ASU Library materials will be shipped by FedEx 2-day express shipping excluding weekends and Federal Holidays. USPS shipping averages approximately 5 business days. Q: What is NOT included in ASU Library’s Home Delivery Program? ASU Library Home Delivery is separate from the following services: Interlibrary Loan (ILL) Home Delivery – For ILL requests, visit: ILL Home Delivery . ASU Bookstore Rental Program – For rental inquiries, visit: ASU Bookstore Rental FAQ .

FAQ Question: How do I change my Interlibrary Loan pick up location in One Search and ILLiad?
Answer: For requests you make within One Search... Within the item record: If the system prompts you to Sign In, click: Sign In (Sign in with your ASURITE ID and password.) When you are signed in, scroll down to the Get It section . In the REQUEST OPTIONS section, click: Request ASU Copy Select a pickup location using the Pickup Location drop-down. (The selected pickup location applies to this request only.) To change your pickup location within your Interlibrary Loan (ILLIAD) account... Access your Interlibrary Loan (ILLIAD) account If the system prompts you to Sign In, click: Sign In (Sign in with your ASURITE ID and password.) On the menu bar of your ILLIAD page, in the Profile section: Click: Update Profile Choose one of the ASU Library locations from the Select a pickup location for Loan pickups dropdown list. At the bottom of the page, save your choice by clicking: Submit Information ( The selected pickup location applies to your requests, with the exception of items requested within One Search as detailed above.)

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: I see "No records found" in One Search after searching a very common topic. What's up?
Answer: If you searched for a keyword that should have yielded results (for instance, searching the keyword Arizona yields millions of records), here are a few things to try: Adjust your search: Check your spelling; replace abbreviations with the actual words they represent. Start with simple search term(s). Start broadly and then narrow your search. Search for partial titles with quotation marks around phrases if the full title doesn't work. Remove punctuation (commas, periods, hyphens, colons, semi-colons, etc.). Still not working? Switch to a different browser to see if that works. Is the Personalization feature turned on? Check the results page (above the section where results would usually show). Clicking the word "Personalized" will turn it off and reset it back to "Personalize". Clear your browser cache (Click the link to learn how to clear your cache.) Then try your search again . If that doesn't work, use the Get Help! feature on our Library Systems Support page. Be sure to include the following information: Which browser you were using (Chrome, Firefox, Internet Explorer, Safari). What computer operating system you were using. And tell us if you were using the ASU Library site from on campus or off campus when you noticed the problem.

FAQ Question: How can a Data Management Plan (DMP) help me?
Answer: Data Management Plans help you... Manage and organize data with an emphasis on: Storage Security File formats Identifiers Naming conventions Provide detailed data documentation Consider which metadata standards to apply Share your data by depositing it in a repository Archive your data with backups and preservation strategies The DMPTool provides templates and guidance to help you write your plan! Click the DMPTool link Then click the "Get Started" icon And select “Arizona State University” as your institution from the drop down list. For additional information or help: Fill out the Library's research service request form to set up a consultation appointment! Watch our tutorials on: Using the DMPTool Research Data Management Basics

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How do I add people outside of ASU to manage my ASU Research Data Repository datasets?
Answer: With our current ASU Research Data Repository authentication process, only submitters with asu.edu emails can create an account. Anyone outside the university can view and access published collections (known as Dataverses), but cannot directly submit/deposit or manage the metadata. If you have members of your project outside of ASU, you can have them submit their datasets and documentation to a secure sharing space like Google Drive or Dropbox and then upload the files on their behalf. If more direct interaction is desired, ASU sponsors can request that collaborators get an “affiliate ID” (ASURITE) to access ASU systems. The ID expires after one year and has to be renewed by the ASU sponsor at that time. To sponsor and request an ASU ID for your collaborators, fill out the ASU Courtesy Affiliate Request - Add or Renew form . Additional workarounds for research teams may include using the Dataverse API , research notebooks like LabArchives , or the OSF as a collaboration platform, and connecting the OSF to Dataverse (which uses an API token). For information on adding co-owners, see our FAQ entry on adding users or co-owners to my datasets .

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: What type of files can I upload to the ASU Research Data Repository?
Answer: The ASU Research Data Repository (powered by Harvard's Dataverse platform) is ideal for depositing and sharing research data and supports a variety of research data types and formats. You are welcome to upload research data, supplementary tables, documentation, publications associated with data, presentations, etc. The Repository supports the uploading of any file type, including shapefiles, images, Flexible Image Transport System files, and compressed files. However, to make use of the Data Explorer feature, you need to upload tabular data files (e.g., Stata, SPSS, R, Excel (xlsx), and CSV). Some files will generate an archival file format for viewing within the repository, but users will have the option to download either the original or archival version. See the Dataverse Platform User Guide for more information.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How do I sign up for (or renew) my New York Times academic group pass?
Answer: ASU Library provides current students, staff, and faculty with complimentary access to The New York Times, including News, Cooking, Wirecutter, and select Games. Access is provided through ASU Single Sign-On (SSO). Eligible users must sign in with their ASURITE@asu.edu email address to access features such as mobile apps, NYT Cooking, and TimesMachine (PDF scans of issues from 1851–2002). Learn how to activate access and troubleshoot issues . Notes: Articles from 1923–1980 are limited to five per user per day. For searchable full-text historical coverage (1851–2021), use ProQuest Historical Newspapers . The Athletic , a separate sports journalism platform owned by The New York Times, requires a separate group pass activation and is also available to current ASU students, staff, and faculty.

FAQ Question: Where can I view materials from Distinctive Collections?
Answer: Please use the Ask an Archivist communication feature to connect with our expert staff to start your research process. We will work with you to schedule an appointment at one of our reading rooms : the Wurzburger Reading Room in Hayden Library or, for Design-related collections, at the Design Special Collections Reading Room at the Design and the Arts Library on the Tempe campus. Appointments can be made to view collections from the Labriola National American Indian Data Center at Fletcher Library on the West Valley campus or in the Wurzburger Reading Room at Hayden Library.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Where can I get support for paying open access article publishing charges (APCs)?
Answer: ASU Library can provide waivers or discounts on APCs with certain publishers as a result of our partnerships and subscriptions. Additionally, we manage the ASU Open Access (OA) Publishing Fund, which supports ASU authors who wish to make their scholarly articles freely available immediately upon publication. All current ASU faculty, staff and students are eligible for funds, with a maximum award of $2000 per request. Applications to the fund will open in January 2026. For a complete current list of partnerships and the criteria and application instructions for the OA Publishing Fund, visit our Researcher Support open access publishing and support page. If making your work open access is important to you, you can publish in a traditional journal as long as you retain the rights to self-archive your work in a repository , such as our KEEP institutional repository or a disciplinary repository of choice.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Can I request textbooks through Interlibrary Loan?
Answer: Interlibrary Loan is available for borrowing items that supplement learning, teaching, or research, not for general course study materials such as textbooks. Due to the high demand of textbook titles both at ASU and other outside institutions, attempts to borrow current editions of textbooks normally takes longer and are frequently unsuccessful. Also, Interlibrary Loan due dates are short and will not cover a full semester use. Instead, we recommend purchasing or renting your textbooks.

FAQ Question: How do I cancel an ILL request? How do I edit a request I already submitted?
Answer: Only requests with the status “Awaiting Request Processing” may be canceled or edited. To cancel/edit a request: Select the appropriate transaction number (TN#). Select "Cancel Request" or “Edit Request” in the top right corner of the page. For requests that have moved out of the status “Awaiting Request Processing”, please contact ILL staff for assistance at ASU-ILL@asu.edu .

FAQ Question: Can I use Respondus Lockdown Browser on library computers?
Answer: Yes, the Respondus Lockdown Browser is installed on all public library computers at all of our library locations, as well as on computers managed at ASU computing sites. To access it, you need to follow these instructions : In the Windows Search bar in the bottom left corner of the screen, search for "Lockdown" (note: searching for "Respondus" will not work.) Click on the link for "Lockdown Browser - Lab Edition" A "Choose Server" pop-up window will appear - select "Canvas at ASU" from the drop-down menu. Log in with your ASURITE ID and password when prompted. Your browser should now be locked and you should see your Canvas courses. Navigate to the appropriate course and quiz/test. To exit the Lockdown Browser, click on the red X in the top right hand corner and answer the prompt as appropriate. Please note that all applications, including Lockdown Browser, are managed by the University Technology Office, not ASU Library. If you have questions or are experiencing technical difficulties using the Lockdown Browser, please contact the UTO Experience Center .

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How can I get help accessing or using Overleaf Professional LaTex editor?
Answer: For help with Overleaf Professional, please visit ASU Research Computing's workshop overview page , which includes office hours, Slack information, and a workshop schedule. You can also check out the Overleaf FAQ and Help page . Access Overleaf at www.overleaf.com/edu/asu#overview . About Overleaf at ASU The Knowledge Enterprise at Arizona State University with W.P. Carey School of Business, Fulton Schools of Engineering, and The College of Liberal Arts and Sciences provide Overleaf Professional for all faculty, students, and staff who want to use a collaborative, online LaTeX editor for their projects. Some benefits of the Overleaf product are that it is a collaborative tool, offers history and track changes features, has a common compiler for reproducibility, offers centralized cloud storage for files, and can be integrated with Dropbox and GitHub. Overleaf is commonly used for: collaborating on research papers, publications, and proposals production of professional-looking mathematical equations automated cross-referencing automatic formatting

FAQ Question: What items can I request through interlibrary loan? What can’t I request?
Answer: You can request these types of items through interlibrary loan: Books Book chapters (up to 3 per book) Journal articles (up to 3 per journal issue) Government documents Theses and dissertations Some media (CDs, DVDs) Some ebooks These items are not available to request: Textbooks Items for personal/non-academic use Entire journal issues Some media (CDs, DVDs) Some ebooks There is no limit to the amount of requests you can submit.  However, we recommend that you only request materials you can use in a reasonable amount of time to avoid overdue fees.  Renewals are not usually available. For more information, contact the ASU Library Interlibrary Loan Department (Monday-Friday, 8am-5pm): (480) 965-3282 Email: ASU-ILL@asu.edu libguides.asu.edu/ill

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: What if the scan I receive is incorrect, missing pages, or illegible?
Answer: Responding directly to your email notification or your service ticket will reopen the request. Please be descriptive in your response as to the specific issue, ensuring ASU Library staff can effectively resolve the issue. Also please note that ILL and digitization services cannot guarantee a pristine or printable copy. Microform sources are analog media formats and are lacking in features considered standard in digital media. Microform formats are fragile, easy to corrupt and mutilate resulting in sub-optimal reproduction. Photographic illustrations reproduce poorly in microform format, with loss of clarity and halftones. Storage and reproduction increase degradation of the film or card source over time.

FAQ Question: How do I gain remote access to ASU Library online resources?
Answer: Remote access to ASU Library online resources is verified by the University’s Human Resource and Student Records and generally granted to: ASU employees Employees who are paid by ASU Human Resources . (Third-party contractors, consultants, and volunteer positions do not qualify for remote access.) Faculty Classified Staff / University Staff Academic Professionals / Service Professionals ASU Undergraduate and ASU Graduate students These students must be currently enrolled in at least one credit-earning course for a degree-seeking or for-credit ASU program. (Students who are members of an academic program, but not enrolled in any courses do not have remote access.) Undergraduate Students Graduate Students / Post Graduate Students Visiting Scholars Courtesy affiliates of ASU Designation by the provost so that non-faculty / students may access ASU services. (Membership in these groups does not guarantee courtesy affiliate status.) Arizona Board of Regents Barrett Summer Scholar Program GCC ASU Partnership Adjunct Faculty Retired Faculty / Retired Staff You will need to sign in with your ASURITE ID and password when prompted. Sign in with your ASURITE ID and password when prompted. From the ASU Library home page at: https://lib.asu.edu The SIGN IN link is located at the top right corner of the page From your MyASU account at: http://my.asu.edu ​ After you sign in to your MyASU account, Click on the Library icon (a stack of books) on the left side of your MyASU home page. The library's remote access policy is available at: https://lib.asu.edu/policies/remote-access

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: If I know the title of an article, how do I find it?
Answer: If you know the title of the article... From the library home page , try the One Search dropdown to either One Search or Google Scholar . Enter the title of the article in the search bar. Put quotation marks around the title to force it to read as a phrase. Click: Search Library Your article title usually appears in the first few results. You can also search for a free (and legal) Open Access copy of the full text through Open Access Button . Current ASU student, faculty or staff: For our full text options, make sure your are signed in to the library system with your ASURITE ID and password. If a full text option is not available, the library will try to get a digital copy of the article for you through your Interlibrary Loan (ILLiad) account. Other researchers: When you are on our ASU campuses, many of our full text online materials may be accessed using our ASU Guest wifi or from an ASU Library guest computer. Or check with your local public library or an academic institution with which you are affiliated. They may have Interlibrary Loan services that serve your needs.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Why do I need to login to the library system?
Answer: Here are a few benefits when you sign in to the ASU Library system before performing a search: If an item is unavailable, logged-in users will see a link to request the item. You can usually Request ASU Copy, Request Chapter Scan, or Request from External Library (Interlibrary Loan). An email will either alert you when the item is ready for pickup or it will contain the digitized article or chapter scan! Logged-in users will see the full results that may also show which years are available for the journal or periodical. Logging in shows your loan period based on your ASU affiliation such as undergraduate, graduate, faculty or staff. You will also be able to see results from our databases , if you are logged in. If you click on the Accounts section of the library home page, it will sign you in to the following accounts: My Interlibrary Loan (ILLIAD) Account My Library Account Where you can view a list of your checked out items, R enew items that are eligible for renewal, And perform other account maintenance tasks. To sign in to the library system: Go to the ASU Library home page . In the upper right corner of the home page, click: Sign In

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Why am I getting inconsistent request options when I click on Get It! @ ASU?
Answer: Different request options appear or do not appear when using Get It! @ ASU, depending on whether you are on or off campus, and whether you have signed in to the library system before you click the Get It! @ ASU icon or link. ON CAMPUS (NOT signed in to ASU Library system) Click: Get It! @ ASU When you see the sign in prompt, click: Sign In If clicking Sign In doesn't automatically log you in... You will be prompted to sign in with your ASURITE ID and password. Click on an option, complete the form, and submit as instructed: For items we own, these options should appear... Request ASU Copy Request Chapter Scan For items we do not own, this option should appear... Request External Copy ON CAMPUS (already signed in to the ASU Library system) Click: Get It! @ ASU If the request options or the sign in option do not appear: Go back and click Get It! @ ASU again. Click on an option, complete the form, and submit as instructed: For items we own, these options should appear... Request ASU Copy Request Chapter Scan For items we do not own, this option should appear... Request External Copy SCREEN SHOTS... ON CAMPUS (NOT signed in to ASU Library system) For an item that we own... If the Sign In option appears : Click to Sign In... For an item we own, these options should appear... Request ASU Copy Request Chapter Scan For an item that we do not own... If the Sign In option appears : Click to Sign In... For an item we do not own... This option should appear Request External Copy MORE SCREEN SHOTS... ON CAMPUS (already signed in to the ASU Library system) OFF CAMPUS (whether you already signed in or not) If the request options or the sign in option do not appear: (This screen shot shows an item we own.) (This screen shot shows an item we do not own.) Go back and click Get It! @ ASU again. This should bring up the request options!

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: I am finishing my ASU dissertation / thesis. Why isn't my remote access working?
Answer: ASU thesis or dissertation writers must be enrolled for at least 1-credit hour at ASU in order to retain online access and checkout privileges: The 1-credit dissertation / thesis course satisfies this requirement. Note: To retain library privileges over the summer, students must pre-register for Fall classes before the end of Spring semester.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Do I need to be an ASU student to use the library? (Visitor Information)
Answer: Public visitors are welcome at any of our eight locations on the four ASU campuses. Hours of operation vary depending on the library and the time of year ( library locations and find contact information ). While you are in the library, you may connect your devices to the ASU Guest wireless network . The ASU Library is open to the public and home to our research collections, open stack collections at every library, and a variety of units and centers designed to engage learning and collaboration for all. Community Borrowing Cards are available for purchase for non-ASU affiliated library users, which provides borrowing privileges for most items from the open stacks collections.  In addition, the ASU Library houses a rich collection of rare, unique and distinctive materials . Get started by speaking with our experts at Ask an Archivist. Please note, building access to our Downtown Phoenix campus Library is limited to current ASU students, staff and faculty with active Sun Cards. Members of the public and Community Borrowers should call 602-496-0300 to arrange a visit. More information can be found on the Services for Visitors web page.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How can I create my own account to Times Higher Education (THE)?
Answer: Current ASU faculty, staff and students can sign up for your own Times Higher Education (THE) account! Registering as a first time user: For anywhere, anytime access to the Times Higher Education, you first need to register using ASU email address. Please follow these instructions so that your personal THE account is associated with the University’s institutional subscription. From the Times Higher Education website click on the user account icon, i.e. the red person icon towards the top right. Register using your Arizona State University email address @asu.edu (Your username will appear in public facing parts of the site such as comments on articles.) Choose a user name and password. Click on Join us at the bottom and you will be logged in. You will receive a confirmation email. Once you have successfully registered your personal account, just click on Login the next time you use the service. Access digital editions: To access digital editions of THE, go to their homepage. Click the Professional tab, then click Digital Editions. Then simply select the issue you want to view. Download the app: The Times Higher Education app is available on iOS, Android and Kindle Fire. Visit your app store provider to download it to your device. Select the edition you would like to view, e.g. UK or Global Log in by clicking on the icon in the top right corner of their home page. Select Account, then click: Existing THE account Enter your username and password.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How do I access government information and government documents?
Answer: ASU Library's Government Documents staff can assist in identifying and locating government information resources. Use the GovDoc-Info form to contact us. ASU students, faculty and staff can request government documents directly from the ASU online catalog . Public and non-ASU users can request government documents, found through the ASU online catalog , by filling out the Request Materials form , and may be picked up by scheduling an appointment using the GovDoc-Info form . Members of the public may use a library workstation to access government information online by scheduling an appointment using the GovDoc-Info form . Are there limitations on access? ALL users must follow acceptable ASU Library rules and procedures. Only ASU affiliates and community borrowing cardholders may borrow / check out our government documents.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Can ASU Library help me with a legal matter?
Answer: ASU Library staff cannot provide legal assistance, such as finding expert witnesses;  interpreting cases, statutes, or regulations; identifying legal forms to use or help in filling out legal forms; and similar activities. We advise consulting an attorney in such cases, and consulting a law library for legal research assistance. ASU Library is separate from ASU Law Library, which provides its own Ask a Law Librarian service.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: I am working on a data management plan. Can you help me identify an appropriate data repository?
Answer: The best place to search for disciplinary repositories is: re3data.org Search tip: Searching for "education" in subjects provides a list of education related repositories . Researchers in the Julie Ann Wrigley Global Institute of Sustainability can also contact the Global Sustainability Support Services for information on GIOS data management. A non-disciplinary option is: Open Science Framework This includes both project management and data sharing. ASU now has an institutional account. To access: Go to osf.io Select sign in (at the top right of the page) Choose "Sign in through my institution" Arizona State is the first option in the drop down list. However, if you have any sensitive information this may not be the final sharing mechanism for you. If you are in the process of writing a plan: DMPTool This tool provides templates for actively building data management plans . Login with your ASU credentials. You can also review our library guide on research data management for additional information, tutorials, and instructions. Note: DMPTool has been updated recently with improved functionality. For additional information or help: Fill out the Library's research service request form to set up a consultation appointment!

Answer: The best place to search for disciplinary repositories is: re3data.org Search tip: Searching for "education" in subjects provides a list of education related repositories . Researchers in the Julie Ann Wrigley Global Institute of Sustainability can also contact the Global Sustainability Support Services for information on GIOS data management. A non-disciplinary option is: Open Science Framework This includes both project management and data sharing. ASU now has an institutional account. To access: Go to osf.io Select sign in (at the top right of the page) Choose "Sign in through my institution" Arizona State is the first option in the drop down list. However, if you have any sensitive information this may not be the final sharing mechanism for you. If you are in the process of writing a plan: DMPTool This tool provides templates for actively building data management plans . Login with your ASU credentials. You can also review our library guide on research data management for additional information, tutorials, and instructions. Note: DMPTool has been updated recently with improved functionality. For additional information or help: Fill out the Library's research service request form to set up a consultation appointment!

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How do I add users or co-owners to my datasets in the ASU Research Data Repository?
Answer: We are using ASU single sign-on as the only method for signing in, and users add their accounts. Library admins cannot add them before they sign in for the first time, and users have read-only access when they are initially added. ASU Library staff provide permissions. Please contact the library and notify us which user you need added, after they have signed into the repository explorer page for the first time. Accounts are created at the time of first sign-in, and after that, the library repository administration team can add them to the account with the appropriate permission levels and even assign them to groups. If you only want to list someone as an author, but not grant permissions to manage your datasets, add their name in the metadata author fields and there is no need for them to sign into the repository. In a nutshell: You invite contributors by giving them the repository link, and they sign in to create an account, and then you request that we grant them access. The library cannot create accounts for them before they sign in. See Roles and Permissions in the Dataverse Platform Users Guide

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: My book has been recalled and I can't return it on time! What can I do?
Answer: All ASU Library items are subject to recall. The recall policy applies to all users and is strictly enforced. It is in effect throughout the year including breaks, sabbaticals and vacations. When a checked out item is requested or recalled, items are due two weeks from the date it was requested by another user. Please review our Recall Policy page and our Returning Library Materials page for details on returning items. If a recalled item is returned after the recall due date, a late fee is charged. If you cannot return a recalled item by the recall due date, return the book to the library as soon as you are able. You may appeal library fines through a written appeal process after the item has been returned . Consult the Billing Appeal Procedure page for more information on how to appeal a fine. For contact information, please review our Billing Contact page.

FAQ Question: What online streaming video platforms are available through ASU Library, and how can I access them?
Answer: ASU Library provides access to thousands of streaming videos through three major providers: Alexander Street Films on Demand Kanopy Streaming videos are available in many other library-licensed collections and open collections. To find streaming videos in these collections, use the A-Z Databases module and limit to Database Type = Streaming Video . Searching this way will allow you to view a list of all of our current databases which contain a significant amount of streaming video content. For faculty, instructors and course designers: All videos in collections licensed by ASU Library can be used in ASU course management shells (Canvas, Learning Studio, Blackboard, etc.), syllabi, documents and other webpages. The ASU Library Resource Organizer is the best method for ensuring consistent links and access. ASU faculty interested in using the library’s streaming course reserve system to digitize video that is otherwise not available online should consult the Streaming Video Reserve Guidelines page. For detailed information about how to use videos in teaching, view this tutorial . Technical assistance: If you are unable to access a video, please submit a request through library systems support . Please indicate the specific video you are unable to access, and include information on your operating platform/device (Mac, PC, tablet, etc.) and browser.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: I just want to find something fun to read.
Answer: Want something fun to read?  Do you have a title or favorite author? Although you can use other methods, we find that the Advanced Search option works best for searching our catalog for a book title or author. Here's how it works... From the library home page https://lib.asu.edu/ Just below and to the right of the main search box, you will find the Advanced Search option: Click:  Advanced Search Change "All resources" to: ASU Library Catalog Change "Any field" to: author or title Change "contains" to: is (exact) or starts with Fill in the blank with the author's name or the title of the book Hit <Enter> on your keyboard to search! If you want to simply search for a broad category of books: Click:  Advanced Search Change "All resources" to: ASU Library Catalog Change "Any field" to: subject Change "contains" to: is (exact) Fill in the blank with the category / genre of book you want, such as: fiction or mystery or romance or science fiction autobiographical fiction or biographical fiction or historical fiction graphic novels or novels or short stories plays or poems or poetry Hit <Enter> on your keyboard to search! Note: If you are looking for FICTION, all of our fiction is arranged by language, then eras, countries, and then by author. The Library of Congress (LC) classification system is designed for academic research rather than for browsing for leisure reading; therefore, LC subject terms are designed to find books by themes and other topics assigned within a literature call number classification.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How long does it take to access materials?
Answer: We ask that you make requests for materials at least five (5) business days in advance, which will allow us to identify, secure, and move items to the appropriate reading room. An archives specialist will respond to all requests within 24 hours (Monday thru Friday, between 8 a.m. and 5 p.m.) to begin the process of fulfilling your request for materials, and will work with you to schedule your appointment. Contact Ask an Archivist for more information.

FAQ Question: I've requested scans of articles from a journal and haven't received a notice that my request was received....
Answer: The screen shot below is the only notification that shows your request has been placed successfully in our queue. (If your place in the queue is 0, no one else is in the queue for that item ahead of you.) To check the status of your requests: At the top of the library home page, click on: Accounts Sign into My Library Account To view all of your ASU Library catalog requests within My Account, click: REQUESTS If you no longer need a request, you can cancel it from the Requests screen.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: What if I don't know the volume, issue, or reel number?
Answer: Here are a couple of things that may help identify information to help fulfill your request: Review the source of your citation for additional information, such as: alternative journal titles and/or updated ISSN Do a search on the internet for article title that may provide a citation. Do a search on the internet for journal title (and alternative journal titles) Occasionally material from older publications may be archived and available for download. If those options don't help you discover useful information to fulfill your request here are a few things to try within our library systems: Navigate to the library home page . Near the center of the home page, choose one of the following options from the drop down list to the right of the large search bar: One Search Then type or paste the title of the article into the search bar. Click SEARCH. If your article is not in the first few results, try a different method. Google Scholar Then type or paste the title of the article into the search bar. Click SEARCH. If your article is not in the first few results, try a different method. Journal Title Then type or paste the name of the journal into the search bar. Click SEARCH. Select the result, if any, for the online version of the journal. If there is a link to the publisher's website , click down the list in the following order until you get to the Table of Contents at the Issue level: Year Volume Issue Look through the Table of Contents listing in page number order. Note: The article you need may actually fall within the pages of a different article. This happens frequently with book reviews!! Open the full text and look for the starting page of your article, just in case it is actually within another article! If you need additional help, try our Ask a Librarian services!

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How will I receive my notifications or scanned request?
Answer: If you've submitted your request via ILLiad : All notifications will be sent to the email address provided in your ILLiad account. Please read the notification carefully as it may provide instructions to access your requested material through alternative means, or it may ask for additional information to continue processing your request. If your request can be fulfilled, the notification will include a link to log into your ILLiad account to obtain your scanned request. If you've submitted your request via a service ticket through the ASU Library Ask a Librarian Service : Your fulfilled request and any notifications will come as replies to your ticket submission. If your request is able to be fulfilled it will be attached as a PDF file.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: I lost library materials that I checked out. What happens next?
Answer: If the item that you lost belongs to the library: The Billing Department will send you a bill with the replacement cost for the lost material. Cost varies by item - Consult with Library Billing Unit. If the item that you lost is an ILL request: For interlibrary loan items that are not returned by the due date, there is an overdue fee of $5.00 per day (starting on the due date) with a maximum overdue fee of $25.00 and a default $150.00 replacement fee. The replacement fee may be adjusted depending on the actual cost of the item billed by the library who owns the item. We have a high overdue fee as many libraries will block us from requesting any materials when their items are returned late. If you find the lost item: You can return items that you have found back to the ASU Library. A full cost of replacement will be refunded if library material is returned in good condition within 6 months of billing. Payments cannot be refunded after 6 months . For more information about lost materials, visit our Overdues, Fines, and Fees website . Library Billing Department: PO BOX 871006 Tempe, AZ 85287-1006 480-965-3417 lillie.johnson@asu.edu

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How do I search for articles in a specific magazine?
Answer: Finding an article - Journal Title Search & search within publication: First, go to Journal Title Search https://lib.asu.edu/find-journals In the top box, type the name of the journal and hit "submit." The results will tell you the dates for any full text coverage in a particular database link. Click on the link to one of the databases that includes the dates for full text for your search needs. Find the SEARCH THIS PUBLICATION option on the resulting page and use keywords to search within this journal. Current ASU students, faculty and staff: If an article is not available as full text, you may wish to request a digital copy of the article through your Interlibrary Loan (ILLiad) account. Other researchers: ASU Library Interlibrary Loan card (ILL card): Here is a link to information about the ASU Library ILL card for guest users https://lib.asu.edu/policies/communityloan/eligibility Or check with your local public library or academic institution with whom you are affiliated; they may have an interlibrary loan service to meet your needs.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How do I search for an article if I have the name of the journal, the year, volume, and issue?
Answer: To find a specific article, use the Journal Title Search to find the journal , then filter within the journal by year, volume and issue: First, go to Journal Title Search https://lib.asu.edu/find-journals In the top box, type the name of the journal and hit "submit." The results will tell you the dates for any full text coverage in a particular database link. Click on the link that contains the date for the article you need. The results will either give you a list of individual years or a group of years. Click on the link that contains the year for your article. The results will usually give you a list of volumes and/or issues. Click on the volume and/or issue you need. When you have reached the issue that you need, the results should be listed in Table of Contents order. Scroll down the list until you find the title (or page number) for your article. Current ASU students, faculty and staff: If an article is not available as full text, you may wish to request a digital copy of the article through your Interlibrary Loan (ILLiad) account. Other researchers: ASU Library Interlibrary Loan card (ILL card): Here is a link to information about the ASU Library ILL card for guest users https://lib.asu.edu/policies/communityloan/eligibility Or check with your local public library or academic institution with whom you are affiliated. They may have an interlibrary loan service to meet your needs.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Who can access full text online?
Answer: Who can access full text online? Current ASU students, faculty and staff: Remember, you must be signed in to access most ASU Library resources from off-campus! Sign in to ASU Library with your ASURITE username and password. The Sign In link is in the upper right corner of the library home page. If you encounter a Sign In reminder within a pale yellow text box on our library pages... Sometimes the library system will prompt you again to Sign In. For additional access or request options, click the "Sign In" link to refresh the sign in connection. Click here for more information on our remote access policy . If you are working for an ASU department, but do not have remote access to ASU Library resources, click here for more information about the process for your designated affiliation. The affiliate status designated by your department controls your ability to access remote library databases and services. Access is not controlled by the library. Arizona residents: Online resources for Arizona residents are available online through the Digital Arizona Library (DAZL) . Note: DAZL is made available by the State of Arizona's Arizona State Library, Archives and Public Records . It is the State of Arizona's library system, which is not affiliated with Arizona State University's ASU Library system.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: I have an ED number from the ERIC database. Is there a quick way to tell if it is available in full text?
Answer: ERIC (Education Resources Information Center) is an education database that is available through ASU Library's online subscription services ( https://libguides.asu.edu/ericproq ) ( https://libguides.asu.edu/ericeduc ), but a public version of ERIC is available at: http://eric.ed.gov Note:  ERIC indexes educational journal articles as well as educational documents.  ERIC documents are assigned an ED number that is followed by 6 digits. There is a quick way to search the public version (ERIC website) to determine whether it contains fulltext of the ERIC digest article / ERIC document. Fill in the blank with the 6 digit ED number and use this URL: http://files.eric.ed.gove/fulltext/ED______.pdf Note: Sometimes authors do not give ERIC permission to post copies of their works, but you can check our ASU Library holdings for a copy of the work. It will not be listed by ED number, but as the actual work such as by title and author.

FAQ Question: Can I use the library system from off campus?
Answer: Visitors: Many online resources provided through the ASU Library’s web site are available to visitors while visiting the library in person by using the ASU Guest wireless network . Please use the ASU for You platform to explore publicly accessible, digital educational resources, curated by the ASU Library, that are available to everyone, everywhere, and can be integrated into your academic and professional work. Find more information on the Services for Visitors web page. Current ASU students, faculty and staff: To access ASU Library resources from off campus, go to the library home page and then Sign In with your ASURITE ID, password and DUO Authentication. The Sign In option is located in the top right corner of the ASU Library home page. If your name is not posted in the top right corner of the library page, click the library's Sign In link. Authenticating gives you more options for full text and other library services! Note: You can also Sign In to your MyASU account to access the library. For more information about accessing full text online from off campus, click the link to view our Can I access full text online? FAQ.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How can I get anywhere anytime access to the Wall Street Journal?
Answer: WSJ.com subscriptions are available to current Arizona State University faculty, staff and students. How to activate your digital subscription Go to the WSJ registration page: WSJ.com/ArizonaState Enter your first and last name Select an Account Type from the dropdown: Student, Professor, or Staff Enter your ASU email address and create a password. The email address and password will allow access on other devices outside of the university network. Click Create to complete registration and create your WSJ subscription. Once you have an account you can go directly to: https://www.wsj.com/ When does my digital subscription expire? If you’re a student: When you activate your student membership, you will be prompted to input their graduation date & year. This is your membership expiration date. If you happen to graduate later than expected and lose access, you can re-activate your membership. If you’re Faculty/Staff: All faculty & staff retain WSJ membership for one year after activating. After that, you will have to re-activate your membership. What if I already have a WSJ subscription? Current subscribers can call 1-800-JOURNAL (1-800-568-7625) to switch from their paid subscription to the membership through their university and they will be refunded the remaining balance of their subscription. What your digital subscription includes Access to wsj.com articles 1997 to present WSJ mobile apps for your device from Apple App Store or Google Play Store . A newsletter of curated content sent to your inbox every month (optional) What about older WSJ articles? Older articles (1984 - present) can be accessed via the Wall Street Journal (ProQuest) database.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How do I fix the "400 Bad Request: Request Header or Cookie Too Large" error when accessing library resources?
Answer: This error often relates to issues with your browser’s cookies or settings. Try the following troubleshooting steps to resolve the problem: Quick Fix. Test in an Incognito/Private Window: Open an incognito or private browsing window and try accessing the resource again. If this works, the issue is likely with your browser’s cookies. Proceed to clear your cache and cookies. Clear Cache and Cookies : Ensure you clear them for all time. Close your browser after clearing, reopen it, log in to MyASU, and try again. Switch Browsers: If the issue persists, try accessing the resource using a different browser. Check Your VPN Connection: Sign out of your VPN and sign back in. Note: Using a VPN does not provide direct access to ASU Library resources. Access is only supported through the ASU Library portal by signing in to your ASU account on the library website or using One Search . Restart Your Device and Network: Disconnect your internet connection. Restart your computer. Reconnect and try again. Check for Blockers: Disable any firewalls, pop-up blockers, or browser extensions that could interfere with access. Try a Different Device or Network: Access the resource from another device or a different internet connection to determine if the issue is device- or network-specific Still having trouble? If these steps do not resolve the issue, please contact us for support. Be sure to include screenshots or a video that shows the full URL in the browser.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: I am signed in but the fulltext of the article won't open for me. What can I do?
Answer: If you are a current ASU student, faculty or staff and the ASU Library system doesn't have an online copy of an article or a book chapter that you need for your research, or if you are signed in to the ASU Library system but the full text access link does open the full text for you... You can request a digital copy through your Interlibrary Loan ILLIAD account! Here is a link to our Interlibrary Loan guide . It includes a link to the sign-in page for your ILLIAD account. Take a look at their FAQs near the bottom of their library guide! They are very helpful. You can also try searching Google Scholar . Sometimes full text can be found by searching within ASU Library's Google Scholar portal. Make sure you are signed in to the ASU Library system. On the library home page, change the dropdown from One Search to Google Scholar. Perform your search and be sure to click the All Versions link below each result that looks promising! The full text often appears in those additional versions. Need more help? Contact us! Email, call, or chat with library staff through Ask a Librarian . Report an error . Include the steps you took to experience the error and include screenshots, if possible.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: My graduate theses or doctoral dissertation is embargoed through ProQuest, but I can do a Google search and download the full document.
Answer: ASU dissertations and theses (in the KEEP institutional repository) are managed solely by the ASU Graduate College. ASU’s policies regarding theses and dissertation embargoes are determined by the ASU Graduate College. Embargoing through ProQuest is separate from embargoing through ASU. Once the ASU embargo expires, full documents are available through the ASU Repository. For information or questions on embargo requests email ASU Graduate College at gradformat@asu.edu Be sure to include: Your full name ASU ID number Year of graduation Title of your document Your email address You can learn more on the embargo process from this article from ASU Graduate College .

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Is there a filter for E-books?
Answer: Note: Ebook collections include many titles that are not owned by ASU Library. (Unless we have purchased a specific title, the ebook you are using could disappear from our collections at any time.) Before assigning an ebook from our collections as your course textbook, check with us to see if the ebook is a permanent part of our collection! You can submit an Ask a Librarian email / ticket. Use ASU Library Tech Support's Get help! button. Or create a library technical support ticket at https://lib.asu.edu/systems/help Here are a few options for searching our ASU Library collections for electronic books... Option 1: From the black header section of the library home page , click: Find In the first column under Materials, click: E-Books Type the book title, keywords, or other criteria into the Library One Search blank and click: Search for ebooks Option 2: Although this is not a one-click filter to limit selections to eBooks, you can: Perform a search using One Search On the results page, use filters on the right side of the page to limit to Availability: Full Text Online Then scroll down the list to Resource Type and select: Books You might need to click Show More to find the Books filter. You can either click Books or check mark the Books filter, then scroll down and click: Apply Your results list should now be limited to eBooks.

FAQ Question: I need articles from specific tourism journals. Where do I start?
Answer: For a list of tourism journals, click on the library guide's tab for: JOURNALS Once you have a journal title or ISSN, go to the library homepage: https://lib.asu.edu Change the One Search dropdown to: Journal Titles . In the search blank, enter the journal title or ISSN and click: Search From the results, select titles that have Online Access Then look for and click the blue links to the databases that have fulltext available. Note: You can search within most of these databases for your tourism topic! For a list of databases that include articles on tourism, click on the tab: ARTICLE DATABASES Among these, try these databases to start: LeisureTourism.com ABI/Inform

FAQ Question: What is the Song Index?
Answer: The Song Index (created by our ASU Music Library staff): allows users to search by song title or composer and will retrieve the name of the anthology and the ASU call number for songs found in score anthologies located at the ASU Music Library . Most items are part of our circulating collection which can be checked out, if you have ASU Library borrowing privileges. The Song Index can be found online at: https://libguides.asu.edu/songidx Note : This index can also be found in our A-Z list of Databases under S for Song Index !

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: What is the best license for my dataset in the ASU Research Data Repository? Should I use CC-by?
Answer: We recommend using CC0 and adding a suggested citation because in most cases data (collection of facts) might not be eligible for copyright protection and ultimately creates an unnecessary barrier to re-use. For further reading see This post from the University of California’s Office of Scholarly Communication provides an overview of the situation and recommends the CC0 license for many kinds of data. The Open Database License (ODC-By) also has an attribution variant. See also our quick guide on licensing and data .

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: I have been using the original Harvard Dataverse. Can my datasets or dataverse be moved to ASU?
Answer: Yes. The ASU Library can work with you to copy your Harvard Dataverse to ASU's Research Data Repository . To maintain your original statistics, we will leave the original datasets in Harvard and create new copies at ASU. A metadata record will point back to the Harvard repository. We welcome ASU researchers who have been using the Harvard interface to work with us, unless there are mitigating circumstances like journal, funder, or collaboration requirements that require Harvard's platform.  Working with our team results in greater documentation and replicability of your work. You can explore datasets in the repository to see examples of datasets curated by our staff. To submit a migration request or have questions about the process, please get in touch with ASU Library's Researcher Support and request a consultation.

FAQ Question: I want to check out some library items. How long can I keep them?
Answer: How long can I check out library materials? ASU Library loan policies. ASU Law Library borrowing policies . Personal Delivery Book Requests: For current ASU students, staff and faculty-all standard loan policies apply. View our Personal Delivery information page . Loan limits: ​Current ASU students, faculty and staff: ​ ASU Library does not limit the number of items that can be checked out to ASU students, faculty or staff. FAQ Question: Can I keep my books longer? What are your policies for renewing library materials?
Answer: Can I renew my library items? ASU Library policies and instructions for renewal: https://lib.asu.edu/policies/renew Items not eligible for renewal include: Materials that were recalled by the library. Materials that have exceeded number of renewals allowed for the material type and/or patron type. Materials checked out with special permission. Materials from areas that do not allow renewals such as videos, microforms, and reserve. Materials obtained through Interlibrary Loan. Additionally, if your borrowing privileges are currently suspended due to library fees, expired registration, etc., renewal will be denied. For current ASU students, faculty, staff and Community Borrowers: All other eligible items are renewed automatically and an unlimited number of times. ​​​Ebooks : Not all e-books can be downloaded. Downloadable e-book are not renewable and must be downloaded again to regain access ASU Law Library Renewals

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Can I still request an Interlibrary Loan if I don't have all of the information about the item?
Answer: Here is a link to our guide about using Interlibrary Loan . It includes a link to the Sign In page for your Interlibrary Loan ILLIAD account. If it is not possible to fill the required fields: Use a " ? " or " n/a " as necessary and use the notes field to include any additional information you have. If you can’t supply the minimum citation information in the ILLIAD request form, please contact Ask a Librarian for help. Tips for getting faster service Search the Library One Search for local holdings by: Title Journal Title Electronic Journal Title Dissertations & Theses Database Provide ISSN/ISBN number or OCLC number If multiple ISSN/ISBN numbers are available, use only one in the ISSN/ISBN form field and place the others in the notes field. If provided, the processing of your request may be automated and not require staff intervention. Verify that your citation information is accurate. Do not abbreviate the title of the publication. Place the citation information in the correct fields on the form. Check your spelling and your citation! Choose the proper request form when submitting your request. Using the proper form will help us find the correct item. Submit each article or book chapter individually on its own request form. Use the Notes field in the request form to provide additional information that may be of use.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How do I add citations to Zotero from Scopus?
Answer: Zotero users at ASU have reported problems adding citations from the Scopus database into Zotero. If you're having difficulties, please try following these steps: Adding Citations to Zotero from Scopus Run your search in Scopus. Select the items from the Scopus result list that you want to put in your Zotero library. Click on Export. Choose RIS format. Open your Zotero library. Choose "File" from menu, then choose "Import". Follow instructions. The items will be added to your Zotero library in a collection with the same name as the file you imported.

Select the items from the Scopus result list that you want to put in your Zotero library. Click on Export. Choose RIS format. Open your Zotero library. Choose "File" from menu, then choose "Import". Follow instructions. The items will be added to your Zotero library in a collection with the same name as the file you imported.

Check your local public library for details about their Culture Pass program!

FAQ Question: When requesting an article/chapter to be scanned from microform, what information do I need to provide to ensure my request is fulfilled?
Answer: Due to quality of source and a lack of indexing, articles and chapters in microform formatting can be difficult to locate. Please provide as much information as possible. A citation and a link to its source is preferable. If a source link is not available, please provide as much of the following information as you have: Publication Title Volume and Issue Number Reel Number Article/Chapter Title Article/Chapter Author Page Numbers
