text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How do I use ASU Library's One Search more effectively?
Answer: Use filters (located on the right side of the results) to see fewer items and items that better match your needs. To filter to ebooks : under Availability, select: Full Text Online under Resource Type, select: Books Other filter settings include: Peer-reviewed journals Available in the library Open Access Library/Location Resource type Subject Author/Creator Publication date Newly added Collection Provider Language Journal title Classification LCC (Library of Congress classification) Searching for a specific item? Add quotation marks around the title to search as a phrase rather than as separate keywords. Try Citation Linker if you have more information about the item. Use Browse to see search results in sorted, ordered lists. You can Browse by: Author Subject Title Call number Use Advanced Search to conduct a more specific search. Search within: Specific fields Subject Title Author Call number Specific time frame Resource type Language Check the Include Non-ASU Resources box to see items we don't own that you can request through Interlibrary Loan . Watch a short tutorial to learn how to use keywords and how to use search connectors better in any database. Sign in to see more results from our subscriptions and submit Interlibrary Loan requests, among other benefits. Save searches (Save Query) and records (use the Pin icon) for easy access and faster searching. Select the Personalize toggle switch (at the top of the search results column) to choose preferred disciplines for your search results. Use the Permalink to save a record for future reference or share it with someone else. After clicking on a result, scroll down to the SEND TO section to find the Permalink for that item! To generate a citation for the item, scroll down to the SEND TO section, and click the quotation mark (") icon . Choose from the following citation styles: APA Chicago/Turabian Harvard 1 MLA (7th or 8th edition) NOTE: Be sure to double-check it and correct any mistakes!

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How do I get a copy of a masters thesis or doctoral dissertation that is not available online?
Answer: Can I get a digitized copy of a thesis or dissertation that is only available in print? If the ASU Library only has paper copies of a thesis or dissertation, and if the item was not submitted by the author to ProQuest Dissertations & Theses... Current ASU students, faculty, or staff: You may submit an Interlibrary Loan (ILLiad) request for the item. We will try to find a copy for you to borrow. ​Lending policy: https://lib.asu.edu/access/lending-policy If it is an ASU masters thesis or doctoral dissertation: You may contact our University Archives with your request. Depending upon copyright restrictions, University Archives may be able to duplicate part or all of a thesis or dissertation with a per page charge plus postage/handling. Email: archives@asu.edu Phone:  480-965-4932 Other researchers: If it is an ASU masters thesis or doctoral dissertation in our University Archives: You may contact University Archives with your request. Depending upon copyright restrictions, University Archives may be able to duplicate part or all of a thesis or dissertation with a per page charge plus postage/handling. Email: archives@asu.edu Phone:  480-965-4932 If it is not a dissertation or thesis in our collections: Check with your local public library or academic institution with whom you are affiliated. They may have an interlibrary loan service to meet your needs.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Where do I pick up ILL items?
Answer: If you live within the Phoenix metropolitan/Maricopa County area , you can pick up your physical Interlibrary Loan items at the ASU Library you select when you submit the ILL request. If you live outside the Phoenix metropolitan/Maricopa County area, and within the United States, you can have physical Interlibrary Loan items delivered to your home address. See our Personal Delivery for Book Requests page for more information. To change your pickup location within your Interlibrary Loan (ILLiad) account: Access your Interlibrary Loan (ILLiad) account . If the system prompts you to sign in, select Sign In. S ign in with your ASURITE ID and password. On the left side of the ILLiad page, in the Tools section, select Change User Information. Choose one of the ASU Library locations from the Select a pickup location for Loan pickups dropdown list. At the bottom of the page, save your choice by selecting Submit Information . The selected pickup location applies to your requests, with the exception of items requested within One Search.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Is there a default or standard metadata schema I can use to describe and organize my research data?
Answer: There is not a default metadata standard for research data. Good planning and consistency with your file names, keywords, names, and dates will result in well-described and well-organized data. This alone may create adequate metadata for your project. There are general and discipline-specific metadata schemas available. Your schema will be informed by the research question you are investigating, your disciplinary focus, or even where you will publish. The DataCite Metadata Schema is a general schema that may be helpful for you. The Data Documentation Initiative (DDI) is an international standard for describing data produced by surveys and other observational methods in social, behavioral, economic, and health sciences. The Digital Curation Centre (DCC) provides a list of disciplinary metadata schema that may be appropriate for your project. To learn more about research data management: Watch the Research Data Management Basics tutorial. Read our library guide on research data management to learn more about metadata best practices and other strategies. For help with your project: Request a consultation by filling out the online request form.

__data__.text:
FAQ Question: Is there a default or standard metadata schema I can use to describe and organize my research data?
Answer: There is not a default metadata standard for research data. Good planning and consistency with your file names, keywords, names, and dates will result in well-described and well-organized data. This alone may create adequate metadata for your project. There are general and discipline-specific metadata schemas available. Your schema will be informed by the research question you are investigating, your disciplinary focus, or even where you will publish. The DataCite Metadata Schema is a general schema that may be helpful for you. The Data Documentation Initiative (DDI) is an international standard for describing data produced by surveys and other observational methods in social, behavioral, economic, and health sciences. The Digital Curation Centre (DCC) provides a list of disciplinary metadata schema that may be appropriate for your project. To learn more about research data management: Watch the Research Data Management Basics tutorial. Read our library guide on research data management to learn more about metadata best practices and other strategies. For help with your project: Request a consultation by filling out the online request form.

FAQ Question: What access does ASU Library have to the Arizona Republic and Arizona Republican?
Answer: The Arizona Republic has been the major newspaper of Arizona for over a century. It was called the Arizona Republican from 1890-1930. Access to ASU Library's subscription databases and Interlibrary Loan/Document Delivery services (mentioned below) require users to be current ASU students, faculty or staff for authentication. Access may also be available over the ASU Guest wifi on ASU campuses for some subscription databases. Arizona Republic Find articles, editorials, notices, and obituaries from the Arizona Republic since 1999, excluding images and ads. Arizona Republic, 1930- on microfilm Especially useful for the years 1930-1998 that are not online or articles that are not online due to the Tasini court case. Current ASU students, faculty and staff may request reels or specific articles through ASU Library's Interlibrary Loan/Document Delivery service. An online index to the Arizona Republic archive is available online at Arizona Republic Historical Newspapers, 1890- where you can find publication dates for their articles; however, full text of the articles requires a subscription to their service. The Arizona Republic archive is not affiliated with ASU or ASU Library. ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Arizona Republican Full-text access to a historical newspaper from Phoenix, Arizona, that became the "Arizona Republic," covering news, politics, business, and culture from 1890 to 1922. Arizona Republican, 1890-1930 on microfilm Especially useful for the years 1923-1930 which are not online. Current ASU students, faculty and staff may request reels or specific articles through ASU Library's Interlibrary Loan/Document Delivery service. An online index to the Arizona Republic archive is available online at Arizona Republic Historical Newspapers, 1890- where you can find publication dates for their articles; however, full text of the articles requires a subscription to their service. The Arizona Republic archive is not affiliated with ASU or ASU Library.

FAQ Question: How do I create Digital Object Identifier (DOI) for my materials?
Answer: Here are a few ways you can obtain a Digital Object Identifier (DOIs) for your materials: Repository Options You can upload your work to a repository that provides DOIs for uploaded works. These can include disciplinary repositories , such as CORE-Humanities Commons or bioRxiv , as well as interdisciplinary options like OSF , or data-specific repositories like DRYAD . This library guide provides more information on selecting an appropriate repository. The ASU Research Data Repository provides registered DOIs for published dataset records produced by ASU Researchers in the repository. ASU Library’s KEEP Institutional Repository provides persistent identifiers called Handles, which are similar to DOIs. Only scholarly works by ASU authors are accepted for both of these ASU repositories. For journals: If you are publishing a journal, the publisher will go through a DOI Registration Agency , such as CrossRef and DataCite in the U.S. For more detailed information about DOI registration and pricing, take a look at the DOI Foundation's Frequently Asked Questions about the DOI System .

FAQ Question: Can I get textbooks for my ASU classes from the library?
Answer: Note: ASU BOOKSTORES: Sun Devil Campus Stores are NOT affiliated with ASU Library. If you wish to buy or rent textbooks for your ASU course(s), go to the Sun Devil Campus Store (ASU Bookstore) website to see which textbooks are available for purchase or rental (physical and/or digital books are included). ASU Library Just remember that ALL library items are subject to Recall. Here is a link to our Recall Policy . Recall Due Dates and Notices When an item is recalled, the due date for that item automatically changes and a Recall Notice is sent. Recall of an item may shorten the original loan period. Recalls are due 3 weeks after the recall request, unless the original due date is within 3 weeks. Due to the strict enforcement of the recall process, failure to receive a recall notice does not absolve the borrower from knowing an item is recalled. If you would like to see if ASU Library has a copy, or access to a copy, of your textbook(s) for use or checkout, follow the steps below. Check our One Search : On the library home page: https://lib.asu.edu In the One Search search bar, enter and search one of the following: Title of book, video, etc. ISBN number for the book Another method is to search the ASU Library Catalog : Change the dropdown from One Search to ASU Library Catalog Enter any one of the following: ISBN number Title of the item Note: Place quotation marks around the title. This forces it to be searched as a phrase. Author of the item Then look through the list of items by that author. Note: Textbooks for your ASU classes are not requestable through Interlibrary Loan .

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: What is a peer-reviewed article?
Answer: Here is a link to our tutorial on this topic: Peer-Reviewed / Refereed Articles Peer reviewed articles are the major articles in academic journals or chapters in scholarly books that have undergone the "peer review" or refereed process before being accepted for publication. They are reviewed by other scholars in the same field or discipline for accuracy and completeness. Remember, only the MAJOR articles in a scholarly journal will be peer-reviewed! The following items in those same types of publications will NOT be peer-reviewed: letters to the editor editorials news

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: My instructor wants us to use credible sources. How do I know what's good?
Answer: Many instructors want you to use factual information that appears in reputable scholarly journals or books rather than opinions or unreliable sources found online from an unknown party.  Most of our library databases let you limit your search to scholarly resources, but even those can include opinionated sections that may not be what your instructor wants. First, make sure you know what types of resources are acceptable for your assignment!  Sometimes newspaper articles are acceptable, but sometimes they are not! Book reviews in a scholarly journal may be ok, but maybe not! So check with your instructor or check your syllabus for specific resource types that are allowed or not allowed. Need to learn how to evaluate resources? Read the script or view the tutorial about Evaluating Resources!

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: What do I do if I received the wrong item through Interlibrary Loan?
Answer: For articles: Reply to the original email notice telling us what the problem is and we’ll ask the lending library to send the correct article. If you've deleted the email already, send an email to ASU-ILL@asu.edu .  Be sure to include the TN# (transaction number). For books or other loans: Please return it to Interlibrary Loan as soon as possible and tell us the problem. You can also call or email us, including the TN# and the problem, at (480) 965-3282 or ASU-ILL@asu.edu . The wrong item will be returned to the lending library and the correct one will be reordered. Interlibrary Loan cannot reorder material until it has been checked in and returned to the owning library.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Why do I need to have good metadata and readme information?
Answer: To assist users in interpreting the data for potential replication or reuse you should add information that helps tell others exactly how you created and used your datasets. For example tabular data can still be hard to read if someone doesn't know what your column headers or variable names mean. Projects are varied and you should not assume someone can just figure out what you did. A readme file, code sheet, or data dictionary can help mitigate confusion and help your data align with our charge to make research data findable, accessible, discoverable, and reusable ( FAIR) . These elements can be uploaded directly into your dataset or added within the metadata. For more information see: How to make a Data Dictionary (Center for Open Science) Guide to writing "readme" style metadata ( Cornell University) ASU Library Data Management Tutorials

FAQ Question: How do I access a thesis from the Barrett Honors College Thesis collection?
Answer: The Barrett, The Honors College Thesis/Creative Project Collection (in our KEEP Institutional Repository ) is directly managed by the Barrett Honors College. Full text access is only available to the ASU Community. If you are not currently affiliated with ASU, you will need to ask permission. ASU employees (faculty and staff) and currently enrolled students may access the full text of a thesis by clicking on the "Sign In" link in the upper right corner of the repository screen and signing in with their ASURITE User ID. They will then be able to view the PDF. Alumni and persons not affiliated with ASU are encouraged to contact thesis@asu.edu for additional assistance.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How do I get the Mendeley Cite plugin for Microsoft Word?
Answer: You can insert references and bibliographies into your document using Mendeley's citation add-in for Microsoft Word. If you are having issues with the Mendeley plugin in Microsoft Word, please try the following things: 1. If you are an ASU student, login to your Microsoft account with asurite @sundevils.asu.edu OR if you are an ASU employee login to your Microsoft account asurite @asurite.asu.edu (employees) (replace asurite with your personal asurite id.) 2. Go to "Apps & Devices" and Install Office (If you already have Office installed, go to step #3) 3. Open Microsoft Word 4. Look for Mendeley Cite in Microsoft's Add-In Store under Admin Managed. You can find the store by going to the “Insert” menu of Word and selecting the downward arrow next to Add-Ins. From the Add-Ins menu, select the Get Add-ins link and search for Mendeley Cite. click the Add button to install the Add-In. After the Mendeley Cite Add-in has been installed, it can be accessed by clicking the Mendeley Cite icon on the Home menu. 3. If you are having issues, please contact the ASU Tech Studio at 1-855-278-5080 or (480) 965-2843

FAQ Question: How do I cite this source in my paper?
Answer: Here are a few helpful sources for citation help. Our ASU Library guides provide information and links to helpful citation resources: Citation Styles Citation Management Tools During our semesters, ASU's University Academic Success Programs offers help through their Writing Centers . The Writing Center resource page includes: a list of ASU writing centers their contact information a link for online writing tutoring and how to book face-to-face writing appointments For tutoring and other inquiries, scroll down ASU's University Academic Success Programs page! It offers help in-person, online, and via zoom or slack. Scroll to the very bottom of their page for tip links!! You might also try Purdue's Online Writing Lab (OWL). For online examples, just search the internet for: OWL MLA style OWL APA style OWL Chicago style OWL AMA style Many of our library databases and discovery tools provide citations for their articles, books, journals, etc. in a variety of citation styles!

FAQ Question: How do I find an item if I already have the full citation - author, title, etc.?
Answer: There are several ways to see if we have the article, book, or other item you need. Use our Citation Linker . Remember to select the appropriate option on the form for: Article (includes DOI search field) Book Journal (includes DOI search field) Search by DOI with the DOI (Digital Object Identifier) Resolver Search by the citation information in ASU Library's One Search * Place quotation marks around the article title so that the words are searched as that particular phrase. Example: " University of transformation: Michael Crow at Arizona State University "

FAQ Question: How do I cancel a request for a book from another ASU library?
Answer: Want to cancel your request? To cancel a request you made for one of our ASU Library items: From the library home page : https://lib.asu.edu In the black header, pause on: Accounts In the drop-down list, click on: My Library Account Sign-in with your ASURITE ID and password, if prompted. Click on: View Holds Select and cancel the items you no longer want. Or you may call the ASU Library and we will cancel your request for you. Call us at: 480-965-3605

FAQ Question: How do I save records from One Search and ASU Library Catalog results?
Answer: When using One Search or ASU Library Catalog... Note: If you are not signed in, saving search queries and results are temporary; items will disappear from the favorites area when you close your browser. To save a search query: Run a search Below the search box, click: Save Query To save individual records: From the results list, use the pin icon to save an item to your Favorites.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Can I browse by call numbers within the library system?
Answer: Yes, you can browse by call number! Go to the ASU Library home page If you are a current ASU student, faculty or staff, Sign In at the top right corner of the library home page; this will give you more retrieval and full text options. Scroll down the page, just below the One Search option, and click: Advanced Search At the top of the resulting page, in the header area, click: Browse Select the appropriate Browse by ______ option from the drop down list. Choices include: Subject Author Title Library of Congress call numbers Call number system used by academic research libraries in the US - These are typically used in our Stacks and throughout many of our other collections. Dewey call numbers Typically these are high school level and under "Children's books" SUDOC call numbers Superintendent of Documents - typically these are US federal document NLM call numbers National Library of Medicine call numbers Other call numbers Many of our specialized collections' items will have "Other" call numbers You may also perform a Virtual Browse by call number around another item in our Catalog: From the ASU Library home page perform a search in One Search Enter keywords, title or author then click: Search On the results page (using the filters on the right side of the page) under Availability, click: Available in the Library Scroll through your results, then click the title of an item On the item record, scroll down to the bottom of the page to the Virtual Browse section. Scroll left and right to see related items organized by call number. Click to view an example of a Virtual Browse result for a sample search: Eco design book

FAQ Question: How do I get an article that is not available online?
Answer: If we do not already have full text of an article or book chapter, here is how you can request a digitized copy of it... Current ASU students, faculty and staff: If we do not have a digital full-text copy of an article, you may request a digital copy using your ASU Library Interlibrary Loan (ILLiad) account. Click here to sign in to your ILLiad account: ILLIAD log in page . For more information, here is a link to our library guide about using Interlibrary Loan: ASU Interlibrary loan Libguide​ . Other researchers: Check with your local public library or academic institution with whom you are affiliated. They may have an interlibrary loan service to meet your needs.

FAQ Question: How can I get help with my ASU homework and research?
Answer: How can I get help with my homework and/or research? ASU support services: ASU provides a variety of support services for ASU students including mentoring, writing support, supplemental instruction and tutoring for specific ASU courses. Visit the Tutoring Center website. You can also visit Student Success Center for more resources. Library research services: To see how the ASU Library can help with your library research, contact the library in person, by email, by phone, etc. Ways to contact us can be found on the Ask a Librarian webpage .

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How do I clear the cache and cookies in my web browser?
Answer: If you're experiencing issues accessing full-text library resources, clearing your browser's cache, cookies, and browsing history may resolve the problem. Follow these steps: Confirm the basics first: Are you a current ASU student, faculty, or staff member? Are you signed in to the ASU Library website ? If you answered "yes" to both, try the steps below to clear your cache. You may need to try both methods if the issue persists. Keyboard Shortcut for Most Browsers If you're using Internet Explorer, Edge, Google Chrome, or Mozilla Firefox, you can quickly clear your cache using a keyboard shortcut: Open your browser. Press Ctrl ( Cmd ) + Shift + Delete simultaneously on your keyboard. This will open a window where you can clear your cache, cookies, and browsing history. Select the appropriate options and proceed to clear the data. For time range, select " All time " or " Everything ." Close/quit the browser and restart it after clearing the cache and cookies. Additional Help by Browser: Clear cache in Chrome Clear cache in Firefox Clear cache in Safari Consider the following additional browser-related settings: Disable Browser Extensions : Some extensions, especially ad-blockers or privacy tools, can interfere with resource access. Temporarily disable them to see if access improves. Update Your Browse r: Ensure you're using the latest version of your browser, as outdated versions may have compatibility issues. Enable Cookies and JavaScript : Many library resources require cookies and JavaScript. Verify that these are enabled in your browser settings. Check Pop-Up Blocker Settings : Some resources open in new windows. Ensure your pop-up blocker is configured to allow pop-ups from library websites. Use a Supported Browser : While most modern browsers are compatible, some resources may work better with specific browsers. If you encounter issues, try accessing the resource using a different browser. Still having trouble? If these steps do not resolve the issue, please contact us for support. Be sure to include screenshots or a video that shows the full URL in the browser.

FAQ Question: I assigned an ebook to my class but it no longer seems to be available. How can I prevent that from happening?
Answer: ASU Library subscribes to a variety of ebook collections. These collections typically include: Books the vendor has selected for inclusion in the database... These titles may be removed from the collection at any time at the discretion of the vendor or publisher. And book titles purchased by ASU Library... These titles are typically a permanent part of our library collection. Note: Before selecting an ebook for inclusion in your course assignments, instructors are advised to submit a Library Technical Support ticket to determine whether the ebook is owned by ASU Library.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How can I activate my New York Times and Wall Street Journal subscriptions that ASU provides?
Answer: Digital subscriptions to the Wall Street Journal and New York Times are available to all current ASU students, faculty and staff. Once you have activated your subscriptions, you can access these news sites via the web and apps for smartphones and tablets. Here's how to activate your free digital subscriptions: New York Times Use this activation link and follow the on-screen instructions to create or renew your account with your ASURITE email (asurite@asu.edu). Recommendation: Use the “Continue with Google” option to sign in. Once your account is activated, visit nytimes.com for direct access. For optimal user experience on mobile devices, once you have activated your NYTimes.com account, download The New York Times News Apps and use the same credentials for the apps. ASU’s Group Access works with the following apps: The New York Times: Live News The Athletic: Sports News NYT Cooking: Recipes & Tips NYT Audio Visit the New York Times Academic Group Pass instructions for additional details and support. The Wall Street Journal ASU provides free access to the Wall Street Journal. Visit the WSJ registration page and select your role (Student, Faculty, or Staff). Complete the registration using your ASURITE email (asurite@asu.edu). After registration, access the Wall Street Journal directly at wsj.com. For optimal user experience on mobile devices, download and use apps for your device from Apple App Store or Google Play Store . Visit the Wall Street Journal Group Pass instructions for additional details and support. Note : If an ASU email has been used previously to create an account, the system may not allow it to be reused for activation. In that case, trying the alternative ASU email format (either asurite@asu.edu or firstname.lastname@asu.edu ) may resolve the issue. Looking for more online newspapers and periodicals? Try this FAQ... What online newspapers and periodicals do you have, and how can I access them?

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: I can't pull up the full text. What's up?
Answer: Many items are simply not available online in full text format. Within our journal subscriptions, some articles may not be available. Why not? An embargo period may be in effect (typically the most recent few months) for which we do not have access. Supplemental or other special issues of a journal may not be available to us as full text. This may be identifiable by a letter accompanying the page number within the citation. Specific articles may be restricted due to lack of copyright permissions from the author(s) of the article. For ASU Library resources available with full text online: Current ASU students, faculty and staff will need to be signed in with their ASURITE ID and password to access most of our full text options. Visitors to our campuses may access most of our online resources on-site using an ASU computer or using our "ASU Guest" WiFi. Click here to view our Remote Access policy . If the full text link takes you to the Journal's home page , try these options: Option 1: Drill down to the article by clicking through the article's year, volume, issue, starting page. Option 2: Look for a "search within this Journal" feature, where you can search for the article title. If you are still not connecting to full text: Current ASU students, faculty and staff may request a copy of the article, chapter, etc. through your Interlibrary Loan ILLIAD account. Our Interlibrary Loan library guide has a link to your ILLIAD account sign-in page. Articles and chapter scans generally take a few hours to a few days to arrive in your email! For help with a particular resource: Contact us through any of the methods listed our on Ask a Librarian page. To report broken links, error messages, and other technical issues: Contact us through the Get Help! icon on the Library Systems Support page.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Where can I publish my work?
Answer: Where can I publish my work? When choosing a venue to publish in, here are some factors to consider: The quality of articles published in past issues of a journal Whether your research fits the scope of the journal Whether the journal is searchable in databases What author rights you want to retain for your work What type of restrictions on access are acceptable to you For more information, take a look at Select a Venue on our Getting Published library guide. If you are concerned about the quality of a journal, our Evaluating Publishing Options page provides a checklist of best practices every reputable publisher should follow. If you are having trouble choosing the right venue for your work, we'd be happy to provide some assistance. Contact Ask a Librarian to schedule an appointment.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Where can I find NSF data management plan templates?
Answer: These resources will help you draft your data management plan DMPTool The DMPTool website provides both examples and templates of NSF data management plans. You can select guidance from up to six different institutions per project. Login with your ASU credentials. The DMPTool provides a couple of generic NSF samples: NSF Generic 1 NSF Generic 2 Note: There are a number of flavors of NSF grants. For the most accurate information, see if the specific grant you are applying for is listed . ASU Research Computing ASU Research Computing can provide you with information on storage and other costs. ASU Research Technology Office has additional resources to review on data management and other technology needs. For additional information: Watch our tutorial on how to use the DMPTool . Our research data management library guide includes tutorials, instructions on getting started with the DMPTool , and other helpful information. For help with your project: Request a consultation by filling out the online request form.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Can ASU students submit their research data to the ASU Research Data Repository?
Answer: The ASU Research Data Repository only accepts datasets submitted by ASU faculty, academic professionals, or university staff. If your request is related to a grant-funded project on behalf of a principal investigator, please have your PI contact us for a consultation to clarify the project's needs. We recommend using the OSF for student-created projects to store and share their work or if you need to publish it immediately. The OSF (Open Science Framework) is a free scholarly web tool with a partnership with ASU to manage various projects. U se your ASURITE to create an OSF account and request an OSF DOI when you publish your datasets. You can view public ASU projects at osf.asu.edu . The page takes a few seconds to load the projects. Note there is a limit of 50GB of data storage for published projects and 5GB for private, unpublished projects.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: What is the maximum file size I can upload to the ASU Research Data Repository?
Answer: Please review the following limitations for large file size upload to the ASU Research Data Repository : Single file upload: The maximum file size limit for individual file upload is 3GB. The experience for each user may vary based on the stability of the internet connection, speed of connection, file complexity, file format, and type; For tabular data files, additional processing by Dataverse means that more time is required to upload and this may result in upload failures depending on the file size, the number of columns, cases or rows, in your file. We recommend you upload files less than 500MB in size that requires this feature (see Dataverse Platform User Guide ); Multiple file upload: The repository accepts .zip or .tar file bundles. Automatically, the repository will unpack your single .zip package file and present individual files contained within to users without any hierarchy or organization maintained. Alternatively, .tar files or double-zipped files can be uploaded directly to Dataverse and will preserve your original file organization. Given the additional processing required for .zip files, we recommend you upload zip files that contain less than 500 files (see Advanced Dataverse Platform User Guide ); If you have many separate data files and/or metadata that you would like to upload to create or populate a Dataset or Dataverse, consider using the SWORD API (see Advanced Dataverse User Guide ), which can support batch upload of files (same size limits as described above). It is recommended that file size be factored in when considering data reuse. Larger files can be more difficult for researchers to handle and work with. Preparing and structuring your data for reuse requires careful consideration of potential use cases, the software required for analysis, the computer system, and file disk space. Dataverse is always developing to support more disciplines and data types, we anticipate that in the future larger datasets will be better supported and managed in Dataverse. If you have questions about file size limits, please contact Researcher Support .

__data__.text:
FAQ Question: What is the maximum file size I can upload to the ASU Research Data Repository?
Answer: Please review the following limitations for large file size upload to the ASU Research Data Repository : Single file upload: The maximum file size limit for individual file upload is 3GB. The experience for each user may vary based on the stability of the internet connection, speed of connection, file complexity, file format, and type; For tabular data files, additional processing by Dataverse means that more time is required to upload and this may result in upload failures depending on the file size, the number of columns, cases or rows, in your file. We recommend you upload files less than 500MB in size that requires this feature (see Dataverse Platform User Guide ); Multiple file upload: The repository accepts .zip or .tar file bundles. Automatically, the repository will unpack your single .zip package file and present individual files contained within to users without any hierarchy or organization maintained. Alternatively, .tar files or double-zipped files can be uploaded directly to Dataverse and will preserve your original file organization. Given the additional processing required for .zip files, we recommend you upload zip files that contain less than 500 files (see Advanced Dataverse Platform User Guide ); If you have many separate data files and/or metadata that you would like to upload to create or populate a Dataset or Dataverse, consider using the SWORD API (see Advanced Dataverse User Guide ), which can support batch upload of files (same size limits as described above). It is recommended that file size be factored in when considering data reuse. Larger files can be more difficult for researchers to handle and work with. Preparing and structuring your data for reuse requires careful consideration of potential use cases, the software required for analysis, the computer system, and file disk space. Dataverse is always developing to support more disciplines and data types, we anticipate that in the future larger datasets will be better supported and managed in Dataverse. If you have questions about file size limits, please contact Researcher Support .

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: What happens to my ASU Research Data Repository account when I leave ASU?
Answer: Users departing the university still have ASURITE identities and single sign-on capability after they leave. However, they do not have access to all ASU provided resources that login gave them access to due to various policies and subscription contracts. We limit access to library subscription resources when you graduate or leave, but our repositories present a unique case where a person might need continued access after separating from the institution. We are still working through this question but if an owner of a collection (also known as a Dataverse) recognizes a user with an ASU ID they may be able to provide continued access for contributing to their respective collections and datasets, but this may not carry over to external contributors who’s temporary IDs have expired and not been renewed.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Looking for Impact Information for articles/books
Answer: There are a few ways you can go about measuring the impact of your work. For article-level metrics, you can look for citation counts. Google Scholar is actually pretty good at this - just look for your article title in Google Scholar and the number of times your article has been cited will be listed in the entry. You can also look for citation counts in Scopus. ImpactStory is also a great service to get some non-traditional impact measurements for your work. Google Scholar is also one of the best ways to get citation counts for books- use the advanced search and languages limit in Settings for reducing the amount of unwanted results. Finally, the Metrics Toolkit can provide some great information about different sources for impact metrics, depending on type of work or measuring your metrics as an author.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: What do I use as my ASU Library card?
Answer: What do I use as my ASU Library card? The library card you use depends upon your relationship with the ASU Library. A photo ID is required to check out materials. Note: If our online system is unavailable, only your ASU Mobile ID or Sun Card can be used for checkout. ​ Current ASU students, faculty, staff and ASU retirees: To checkout library items, use: Your ASU ID card (ASU Mobile ID or Sun Card; these are photo IDs); OR your ASU ID number, along with your government issued photo ID. ASU Library Community Card holders: To checkout library items, use: Your ASU Library Community card, along with your government issued photo ID; OR your ASU Community patron ID number, along with your government issued photo ID.

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: What online newspapers and periodicals do you have, and how can I access them?
Answer: ASU Library provides free digital access to several major newspapers for current ASU faculty, staff, and students. Follow the instructions below to activate and access these subscriptions: The Athletic Visit this activation link and follow the on-screen instructions to create or renew your account with your ASURITE email (asurite@asu.edu). Important : Log out of any existing The Athletic account before activating your pass. Recommendation: Use the “Continue with Google” option to sign in. Once your account is activated, visit theathletic.com for direct access. Visit The Athletic Group Pass instructions for additional details and support. The Chronicle of Higher Education Sign into MyASU with your ASURITE ID and password. Visit this proxied link to access The Chronicle of Higher Education. Optional: Create a free personal account using your ASURITE email (asurite@asu.edu) to access chronicle.com from anywhere. The Economist Sign into MyASU with your ASURITE ID and password. Visit this proxied link to access The Economist. The New York Times Use this activation link and follow the on-screen instructions to create or renew your account with your ASURITE email (asurite@asu.edu). Recommendation: Use the “Continue with Google” option to sign in. Once your account is activated, visit nytimes.com for direct access. Visit the New York Times Academic Group Pass instructions for additional details and support. The Wall Street Journal ASU provides free access to the Wall Street Journal. Visit the WSJ registration page and select your role (Student, Faculty, or Staff). Complete the registration using your ASURITE email (asurite@asu.edu). After registration, access the Wall Street Journal directly at wsj.com. Additional Newspapers and Periodicals ASU Library offers access to thousands of other journals, newspapers, and periodicals. Sign into MyASU with your ASURITE ID and password for remote access. To browse or search, go to: Journal Search : Look up specific journals, newspapers, and periodicals. A-Z Databases : Locate specialized databases by Database Types (e.g., News & Newspaper Articles, E-Journal Collections, E-Book Collections). Troubleshooting and Support If you encounter issues accessing full-text articles or subscriptions: If an ASU email has been used previously to create an account, the system may not allow it to be reused for activation. In that case, trying the alternative ASU email format (either asurite@asu.edu or firstname.lastname@asu.edu ) may resolve the issue. Review the library’s FAQ on journal access. Contact ASU Library for assistance: Submit a ticket : Ask a Librarian Phone: 480-965-3605 Text: 480-525-9826 Helpful FAQs: Journal Title searching Full text access Who can access full text? What if I can't pull up the full text? (What can I do? What are my options?)

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: Are there due dates or fees for interlibrary loan items? Can I renew ILL items?
Answer: Yes, interlibrary loan items have due dates and overdue fees.  These differ depending on the item type and library who is lending the item to you. Due dates For physical items, you can check the due date on the strap attached to the item, or through your ILLiad account .  The average loan period for most physical items is between 4 and 8 weeks. Electronically delivered items like articles and ebooks do not need to be returned.  Your access will automatically expire after 60 days. Overdue fees Most ILL items are free to request, receive, and return. For interlibrary loan items that are not returned by the due date, there is a  there is a $5 per day late fee with a maximum overdue fee of $25, and a default $150.00 replacement fee. The replacement fee may be adjusted depending on the actual cost of the item billed by the library who owns the item.  We have a high overdue fee as many libraries will block us from requesting any materials when their items are returned late. Home Delivery items are shipped FedEx 2 Day, and include a FedEx Ground return label.  You are responsible for overdue, damaged, and lost items. Renewals Renewals are usually not available for interlibrary loan items. To have ILL staff negotiate an extension on a due date with the owning library, please contact us prior to the due date on the item. For more information, contact the ASU Library Interlibrary Loan Department (Monday-Friday, 8am-5pm): (480) 965-3282 Email: ASU-ILL@asu.edu libguides.asu.edu/ill

text_resource.text:
FAQ Question: How do I access an ASU dissertation or thesis?
Answer: How do I access an ASU dissertation or thesis? Current ASU students, faculty and staff: The availability, location, and format of ASU dissertations and theses depends on the time period in which the student completed their degree. Check our ASU Dissertations and Theses library guide for more information on where to search. Other researchers: You can borrow copies of most of our dissertations and theses through your institutional library . We lend to academic libraries, public libraries and corporate libraries. More information about lending to other institutions can be found in our Interlibrary Loan library guide. You can also search the ProQuest Dissertation Express service to see if they have a copy of the item you need. Or you may contact University Archives to see if we have a copy. Our University Archives may be able to duplicate the entirety of a thesis or dissertation with a per page charge plus postage/handling. Email: archives@asu.edu For more information on obtaining ASU Dissertations and Theses, see our ASU Dissertations and Theses library guide.

text_resource.text:
Database: Archives of the Church of Uganda Online
Description: Records in this collection document the history of the Church of the Province of Uganda, including some of the first written documents about and originating from Uganda. It covers the period from the arrival of the first Church Missionary Society missionaries at King Mutesa's court (1877) to the early 1980s. Contents of the collection include legal and administrative documents, correspondence, publications, personal records and more from the Offices of the Archbishops and Bishops of Uganda, the Education Secretary General, the General, Financial, and Provincial Secretaries, the Provincial Treasurer, and the Mother’s Union. The collection documents are in English and Bantu languages.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/archchurchuganda

text_resource.text:
Database: ASU School of Music Performance Archive
Description: The ASU School of Music Performance Archive is devoted to the preservation and dissemination of recordings of performances offered by the School of Music from 1985 to the present day. Content is derived from undergraduate and graduate student recitals, faculty performances, and concerts given by the School’s various ensembles. It provides streaming access to a wide range of music, some of which has never been available on commercial sound recordings. In creating this archive, it is the intent of the ASU Library to provide a freely available, valuable, electronic resource for the scholarly study of music.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/asumusicperf

text_resource.text:
Database: ASU Sheet Music Collection
Description: The Sheet Music Collection consists of approximately 30,000 pieces of uncataloged sheet music ranging from the late 1800s through the 1980s. The sheet music in the digital repository is presented as part of the historical record. The topics, illustrations, and language reflect the attitudes and beliefs of earlier times. Arizona State University does not endorse the views expressed in these materials that may contain images and text that are offensive to some users.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/ASUSMC

Database: CFile Daily
Description: Cfile Foundation was founded in 2013 to provide an expanded ceramic field— art, design, architecture and technology—with two things: a knowledge center where the new paradigms shaping our future (and that of other arts) can be addressed, published and broadcast; and a virtual place where a new global, inter-disciplinary community can grow, combine its resources and serve the field during a time of unparalleled flux. In essence we set out to make a ceramic GPS device that would guide the field to its future.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/cfileonline

text_resource.text:
Database: China Data Online
Description: Coverage:1949+Maximum Concurrent Users:UnlimitedChina Data Online includes China Geo-Explorer I and China Geo-Explorer II. China Geo-Explorer is a web-based spatial data service that allows easy access to a rich collection of unique, authoritative, and comprehensive information from government statistics, population and economic censuses, and many other data sources in a spatially integrated system with many powerful functions for exploratory spatial data analysis. Unlike many other GIS systems and mapping tools, China Geo-Explorer is compatible with most web browsers and does not require advanced GIS skills to learn and operate. With this spatial intelligence, users can generate maps, charts, tables, and reports using demographic and economic data from China for regional planning, business investment, population, housing, environmental assessment, public health and many other spatial studies. Data sources in this database include demographic and business information for all geographies in the People’s Republic of China (PRC), covering 31 provinces, 345 prefecture cities, 2,873 counties, and over 50,000 townships.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/chinadata

text_resource.text:
Database: Digital Theatre+
Description: A digital streaming collection of over 1000 full-length films of Shakespeare plays, modern dramas, classical music, dance, musicals, opera and adaptations of classic novels. Digital Theatre Plus includes behind-the-scenes documentaries as well as teaching and learning resources to facilitate a deeper understanding of the productions and texts. Learning resources include a detailed introduction, plot summary, character biographies, a relationship map, language analysis, scene study, performance background and historical context for each play.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/digitaltheatre

Database: Disabilities in Society, Seventeenth to Twentieth Century (History of Disabilities) (Gale Primary Sources)
Description: A comprehensive collection that examines how society has historically treated and perceived individuals with disabilities, featuring personal memoirs, treatment records, educational methods, and organizational reports. It highlights institutional discrimination, social control, and political exclusion, while showcasing the efforts towards rehabilitation, independence, and the progress of disabled individuals​
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/history-disabilities-20cent

text_resource.text:
Database: Early American Newspapers, Series 1: From Colonies to Nation
Description: Early American Newspapers, Series 1, 1690-1876 offers 350,000 fully searchable issues from over 710 historical American newspapers. Focusing largely on the 18th and early 19th centuries, this online collection is based on Brigham’s “History and Bibliography of American Newspapers, 1690-1820” and provides unprecedented access to the nation’s early periods. Additionally, EAN, Series I is cross-searchable with other Archive of Americana® collections held by ASU Library.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/ealryamernews

text_resource.text:
Database: eHRAF Archaeology
Description: Coverage:1975+Maximum Concurrent Users:UnlimitedeHRAF Archaeology is a cross-cultural database with information on the prehistory of the world.  It is organized by regions and archaeological traditions, and each tradition consists of a general summary and full text documents.  The full-text sources are subject-indexed at the paragraph level, and include books, journal articles, dissertations, and manuscripts resources. eHRAF Archaeology is a unique resource designed to facilitate comparative archaeological studies and research.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/ehrafarchaeology

__data__.text:
Database: eHRAF Archaeology
Description: Coverage:1975+Maximum Concurrent Users:UnlimitedeHRAF Archaeology is a cross-cultural database with information on the prehistory of the world.  It is organized by regions and archaeological traditions, and each tradition consists of a general summary and full text documents.  The full-text sources are subject-indexed at the paragraph level, and include books, journal articles, dissertations, and manuscripts resources. eHRAF Archaeology is a unique resource designed to facilitate comparative archaeological studies and research.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/ehrafarchaeology

text_resource.text:
Database: eHRAF World Cultures
Description: Coverage:1800+Maximum Concurrent Users:UnlimitedeHRAF World Cultures database is a collection of thousands of books and articles on cultures from all regions of the world. eHRAF is interdisciplinary and provides ethnographic research for anyone interested in specific data on a particular ethnic group, culture or country, and for researchers investigating topics like kinship, or settlement patterns on a comparative cross-cultural basis.  The database is unique in that sources are subject-indexed at the paragraph level, which allows precise searching for concepts not easily found with keywords.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/ehrafworldcultures

text_resource.text:
Database: Eight Centuries
Description: Coverage:1106-1960Maximum Concurrent Users:UnlimitedEight Centuries (formerly 19th Century Masterfile) is a database covering source material dating from 1106 until 1960 (varies by source). 8C aggregates indexes, catalogs, collections, and other finding aids, and includes citations to 9,000 periodicals in 30+ languages. 8C provides access to articles, newspapers, books, U.S. patents, government documents, and images. Links to open access and subscription full-text sources are included where available.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/8centuries

Database: Entertainment Industry Magazine Archive
Description: Coverage:1880 – 2015EIMA is a digitized collection of primary source American and British periodicals focused on broadcasting, film, music, radio, television, theater and video games. Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/proquesteima

text_resource.text:
Database: eStatement Studies
Description: Maximum Concurrent Users:UnlimitedProvides annual financial ratios benchmarks (FRB) and industry default probabilities (IDP) for NAICS industry classifications. The data is presented for each industry by asset size, sales, and historical 5-year comparisons of totals. Used to compare a company's performance against its industry/competitors, see trends in industry performance over time, or assess credit risk. The Library's access is to eStatement Studies content only.  Content in other products shown on the website is NOT available.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/estatement

Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/feminismcuba1920

text_resource.text:
Database: FIAF International Index to Film Periodicals Database
Description: Coverage:1972+Maximum Concurrent Users:UnlimitedThis database contains over 500,000 article citations from more than 345 periodicals. It offers in-depth coverage of the world's foremost academic and popular film journals, many of which are seamlessly linked to a growing library of film journals. Each entry consists of a full bibliographic description, an abstract and comprehensive headings (biographical names, film titles and general subjects). Also included is The Treasures from the Film Archives dataset; a database containing credits and holdings information about the silent-era film holdings of film archives from around the world.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/fiaffilm

Description: Coverage:Updated continuallyMaximum Concurrent Users:UnlimitedFoundation Directory Online provides the most accurate details available on U.S. funders and their grants. text_resource.text:
Database: GeoScienceWorld
Description: GeoScienceWorld is a nonprofit collaborative and comprehensive resource for research and communications in the earth sciences. Searchable full text is integrated with GeoRef, a comprehensive indexing and abstracting database for information from geosciences journals, conference, dissertation and other resources. Coverage for GSW journals is mostly 2000 to present, with records from GeoRef covering geosciences literature from the late 1700's to present. Advanced search capabilities, including geographic searching by latitude and longitude, variety of options for limiting results sets, and other features are available.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/geoscienceworld

Database: Hemispheric Institute
Description: Maximum Concurrent Users:UnlimitedThe Hemispheric Institute website houses a vast collection of invaluable materials related to performance in the Americas, including online archives of hundreds of hours of video footage; a series of multimedia casebooks devoted to specific artists, companies or themes; as well as curated pedagogical modules that include scholarly texts, course syllabi, interviews, image galleries, and more. Materials are in English, Spanish and Portuguese.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/hemispherperf

Database: Historical Abstracts with Full Text
Description: Database covers the history of the world, excluding the United States and Canada, from 1450 to present with indexing to historical articles from more than 1800 journals in over 40 languages published since 1955. The related disciplines of archeology, anthropology and sociology are also covered.

text_resource.text:
Database: History of Christianity
Description: Maximum Concurrent Users:UnlimitedThis 6 part streaming video series examines the origins of Christianity and delves into what it means to be a Christian. The series uncovers how a small Jewish sect that preached humility became the biggest religion in the world. The series includes subjects from Palestine in the first century to India in the third, from Damascus to China in the seventh century and from San Francisco to Korea in the twentieth. The series is a BBC co-production with the Open University and Jerusalem Productions.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/histchristianity

text_resource.text:
Database: Index Islamicus
Description: Coverage:1906+Maximum Concurrent Users:UnlimitedIndex Islamicus database indexes literature on Islam, the Middle East and the Muslim world. It is produced by the Islamic Bibliography Unit at CambridgeUniversity Library. The Unit was established in 1983 to continue the compilations and publications of the Index Islamicus bibliography and to transmit knowledge about Islamic and Middle East studies, which have been part of the curriculum at CambridgeUniversity since the early 17th century. Records included in the database cover almost a century of publications, with some going back to 1906. Material cited in the Index Islamicus includes not only work written about the Middle East, but also about the other main Muslim areas of Asia and Africa, plus Muslim minorities elsewhere. Over 2,000 journals are monitored for inclusion in the database, together with conference proceedings, monographs, multi-authored works and book reviews. Journals and books are indexed down to the article and chapter level.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/idxislamicus

text_resource.text:
Database: International Bibliography of Art (IBA)
Description: Maximum Concurrent Users:UnlimitedIBA provides authoritative coverage of international scholarship:  European art from late antiquity to the present, American art from the colonial era to the present, and global art since 1945.  Visual arts in all media are covered: painting, sculpture, drawing, video, installations, new media, decorative and applied arts, museum studies and conservation, archaeology, folk art and material culture, classical studies, antiques, architectural history.  It covers core journals, plus detailed coverage of monographs, essay collections, conference proceedings and exhibition catalogues. IBA is the successor to the Bibliography of the History of Art (BHA), and includes records created by the Getty Research Institute in 2008-09.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/iba

text_resource.text:
Database: Krasnyi Arkhiv Digital Archive (DA-KAR)
Description: Krasnyi arkhiv was a scholarly historical journal published in Moscow in 1922-1941. Published important archival materials on the history of Tsarist Russia and the early years of the Soviet Union. The Krasnyi Arkhiv. Digital Archive contains the entire collection (106 volumes, 1,010 articles, more than 23,000 pages), completely digitized with full-image browsing and full-text article-level search and browse functions.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/krasnyi

text_resource.text:
Database: Life and Times of Jorge Luis Borges
Description: Coverage:1923+Maximum Concurrent Users:UnlimitedThis database contains the largest collection of materials related to one of the 20th centurie’s most important literary, intellectual, and cultural figures. Includes a complete primary bibliography of his works, a secondary bibliography of articles about Borges, interviews, criticism, and news articles. Full-text for many of the articles about Borges is included. Although the full-text of Borges' works is not included, the database provides a full-text search capability for these texts with information on how to locate the full-text for works cited. Navigational menus are in Spanish only.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/jorgeluisborges

__data__.text:
Database: Life and Times of Jorge Luis Borges

Full-text for many of the articles about Borges is included. It includes works by major legal theorists such as Grotius and Vattel, as well as foreign legal treatises, comparative law texts, and international law resources. Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/mmlprimary

text_resource.text:
Database: Making of Modern Law: U.S. Supreme Court Records and Briefs, 1832--1978 (Gale Primary Sources)
Description: Contains the records and briefs brought before the U.S. Supreme Court from 1832 to 1978. The collection is derived from two reference sources. For 1832 through 1915, the documents are based primarily on the holdings of the Jenkins Memorial Law Library in Philadelphia. For 1915 to 1978, the source is the Library of the Association of the Bar of the City of New York.
Link: https://libguides.asu.edu/mmlusct
